studyMetadata.title: BUILD 01: 2 Timothy & the Pastoral Epistles
studyMetadata.subtitle: Preaching the Gospel & Godly Leadership
studyMetadata.description: A fifteen-unit church leadership training module exploring 2 Timothy and the Pastoral Epistles, developing skills in Bible handling, preaching, theological reflection, and gospel-centred ministry.
studyMetadata.author: Canon Dr Jem Hovil with Canon Stephen Ssenyonjo Kewaza

elements.text:
Welcome to BUILD Module One — 2 Timothy and the Pastoral Epistles: Preaching the Gospel and Godly Leadership.

This is the Manual for Participants. It contains everything you need to work through the fifteen units of Module One — whether on your own, with a friend, or in a local BUILD group. The units will take you through 2 Timothy and the Pastoral Epistles, equip you with tools for handling the Bible, and develop your understanding of the gospel and your practice as a leader.
Introducing BUILD

Most church leaders in the world today serve the growing churches of the majority world. It is impossible to overstate the significance of these local leaders for the current health and future prospects of the worldwide church — yet most have received little or no training.

In response, BUILD equips leaders to grow healthy churches. It does so with confidence because Jesus made a promise: ’I will build my church’ (Matthew 16:18).

elements.question: Before you begin Module One, take a moment to reflect: where are you now as a church leader? What training have you received — and what gaps do you most feel?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection — be honest about where you are starting from…

elements.text:
BUILD stands for Biblical Understanding for In-service Leadership Development. It grew out of the Church of Uganda’s response to its training needs and is first and foremost a grassroots church leadership training initiative. BUILD has its own course and materials — designed and developed in Uganda — which deliver biblical, theological, and practical education by training trainers to use its curriculum.

This multiplication model means BUILD can have an extensive impact. It is used by churches in similar contexts across East Africa and beyond, and a regional BUILD network has grown as a result.
How BUILD Was Developed

The BUILD curriculum was compiled primarily by Canon Dr Jem Hovil with Canon Stephen Ssenyonjo Kewaza. But the materials capture the input and perspectives of many individuals — the bulk of the work was carried out through ten participatory curriculum development workshops hosted by Uganda Martyrs Seminary, Namugongo, from 2009 to 2014. Those workshops brought together participants from Uganda, South Sudan, Rwanda, Tanzania, and Kenya, while others from Burundi and Congo contributed later. The workshops were based on earlier research that included 573 survey responses.

Special mention goes to Mr Daniel Mukiibi Muwanguzi in Uganda, who was instrumental in shaping the training there and seeing it through accreditation, and to Canon Capt Benjamin Kibara Wathituni, who was key to a similar process in Kenya and critical in the ongoing revision and translation of materials.

elements.term: The Most Reverend Henry Luke Orombi, seventh Archbishop of the Church of Uganda
elements.question: What did Archbishop Orombi say when commending and launching the BUILD programme?
elements.answer: "The COU is very grateful for the BUILD initiative, which has given us simple, practical and effective tools for equipping our multitude of workers on the ground. Many leaders need training brought to them and ongoing encouragement in the midst of the challenges of serving Jesus Christ today. BUILD is meeting those needs with its faithfulness to the truth and teaching of the Bible, and is bringing change to people’s lives, churches and communities. All this is in keeping with the COU’s core values, such as selfless service. The COU is very proud of this home grown resource, which we have had such a hand in developing. And we now praise God for the way in which he is using BUILD to strengthen and multiply church leaders in our neighbouring countries and beyond. We trust that you will find BUILD as useful and instructive as we do, and may God continue to bless this work and build his church, to the praise and glory of his name."

elements.question: Archbishop Orombi says BUILD meets leaders’ needs ’with its faithfulness to the truth and teaching of the Bible.’ Why do you think faithfulness to the Bible is the foundation he highlights — rather than, say, practical skills or organisational methods?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: How to Use This Manual

Module One contains fifteen learning units. Each unit is one of four types: Tools (for handling the Bible), Encounters (applying those tools to Scripture passages), Reflection (thinking through issues for yourself), and Action (practical training in ministry skills). Unit 1 explains these in detail.

As you work through each unit you will find:
  • Bible passages to read carefully before answering the questions that follow.
  • Questions to answer in your own words — with hints available if you need a prompt, and sample answers to compare with your own after you have tried.
  • Callouts you can tap to expand — containing frameworks, worked examples, and reference material.
  • Reflection questions for personal and honest engagement with what you are learning.
Work through each unit at your own pace. Take time with the questions — the goal is not to get through quickly but to understand deeply and grow genuinely.

elements.text:
We pray that you will be built up through BUILD — and go on to build up many others in their faith and in their faithfulness as leaders in God’s Church.

Begin with Unit 1, which introduces BUILD in more detail and helps you catch its vision. From there, each unit builds on the one before. You are following in the footsteps of Timothy — called to be a worker who correctly handles the word of truth (2 Tim 2:15). May God use these materials to equip you well.

Welcome to Module One of the BUILD course — 2 Timothy and the Pastoral Epistles: Preaching the Gospel and Godly Leadership. This unit introduces you to BUILD and helps you catch its vision for ’Building leaders, building churches, building the kingdom.’ It will also help you understand the BUILD course you are beginning and the BUILD programme you are part of.

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Explain the importance of BUILD to another church leader
2. Make an informed commitment to continuing with BUILD learning
3. Recall a verse that expresses what BUILD stands for
4. Demonstrate an awareness of the challenges you face in your ministry
5. List and describe the key features of the BUILD curriculum
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.text: 1.1 BUILD’s Logo
A logo is a picture or symbol that communicates what something stands for. BUILD’s logo will help us to see what BUILD is about. Look at it carefully before answering the questions below.

elements.alt: The BUILD logo: a stylised church building outline with a cross at the top, the word BUILD in bold letters in the centre, and an open Bible at the base.
elements.caption: The BUILD logo

elements.question: When you look at the BUILD logo, what things do you see? What do you think each element means, and what might it tell us about BUILD and about a good church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Describe what you see — the shapes, symbols, and words. Then think about what each one might represent…
elements.sampleAnswer: The logo contains several distinct elements. The church building shape — a house outline with a pointed roof — speaks of the local church as the home and context for BUILD’s work. The cross at the top of the building points to the centrality of Christ and his gospel: the church exists because of the cross, and everything BUILD does flows from it. The word BUILD in bold letters at the centre names the programme and its purpose — building. And the open Bible at the base speaks of Scripture as the foundation on which everything rests: leaders, churches, and the programme itself are all built on God’s word. Together they suggest that a good church is one that is centred on the cross, founded on the Bible, and committed to building — leaders, congregations, and God’s kingdom.
elements.questionHint: Look at each element in turn: the shape of the building, the cross at the top, the word BUILD, and the open book at the base. What does each one communicate on its own — and what do they say together?

elements.question: Think about your own church. To what extent does it display the marks suggested by the BUILD logo — and where does it fall short?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Be honest — where does your church reflect these values well, and where are there gaps?
elements.sampleAnswer: This question is designed to provoke honest self-assessment rather than produce a ’correct’ answer. A helpful framework: consider the six marks of BUILD identified from the logo — the centrality of God’s gospel, the foundation of God’s word, the presence of God’s Holy Spirit, the value of God’s people, the priority of God’s mission, and the importance of godly leaders. For each one, ask: does our church display this mark visibly and consistently? Where are we strong, and where are we weak? The goal is not to discourage but to identify where BUILD’s training is most needed and where it can make the greatest difference.
elements.questionHint: Try to be specific rather than general. Instead of ’we are pretty biblical’, try to name a concrete example of where God’s word shapes your church’s life — or a concrete example of where it doesn’t.

elements.text:
In looking at BUILD’s logo we can see some of the things that are most important to BUILD:
  • The centrality of God’s gospel
  • The foundation of God’s word
  • The presence of God’s Holy Spirit
  • The value of God’s people
  • The priority of God’s mission
  • The importance of godly leaders who encourage all of the above
1.2 BUILD’s Aim and Anchor Verse

The word BUILD sums up the aim of the programme: ’To BUILD God’s Church through helping to grow and multiply healthy local churches.’ Everything else about the programme serves that purpose — keeping God’s true gospel central, building on the foundation of God’s word, welcoming the presence of his Holy Spirit, loving God’s people, prioritising his mission, and training local leaders to facilitate all of this.

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overpower it.

elements.text:
The BUILD anchor verse is Matthew 16:18. The key phrase is: ’I will build my church.’ Learn the whole verse if you have not already done so.

Peter was the first disciple to recognise and proclaim that Jesus is the Christ — ‘the Messiah, the Son of the living God’ (Matt 16:16). Christ’s church is built on the witness of the apostles and the good news they proclaimed. God’s church will grow because Jesus himself has promised to build it — he will nurture and protect it until it is firm and steadfast. In Module One we will see that those first leaders passed on the baton to others, who passed the message on to others, and it has now reached us (2 Tim 2:2).

In Christ’s strength, with the same gospel, and motivated by his promise, our job is to make sure that chain of witness and growth continues. It is one of the greatest privileges on earth to serve as a local church worker or church leader — whether as a volunteer, part-time, or full-time. BUILD will help us to become better builders with Christ and for him.

elements.question: What does it mean for the health of your ministry to know that Jesus himself has promised to build his church? How does Matthew 16:18 change the way you think about your role as a leader?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the difference between building something yourself and being a co-worker with someone who has already promised it will succeed…
elements.sampleAnswer: Knowing that Jesus has promised to build his church fundamentally reframes the role of a church leader. We are not the primary builders — he is. This means that the health and growth of the church does not ultimately rest on our abilities, our strategies, or our hard work alone. It relieves a crushing burden that many leaders carry: the feeling that everything depends on them. At the same time it gives us a secure foundation for confidence and perseverance — not in ourselves, but in his promise. Leaders who grasp this tend to pray more, trust more, and burn out less. They work hard, but they work as co-labourers with someone who cannot fail.
elements.questionHint: Think about the pressures you feel as a leader. How many of them come from feeling that the church’s health and growth depends entirely on you? What changes if Jesus is the one doing the building, and you are one of his tools?

elements.text: 1.3 The Five Words BUILD Stands For

Each letter in the name BUILD begins a word that is important to the programme. Together they form the full name of what BUILD stands for: Biblical Understanding for In-service Leadership Development. Each word has been linked to a verse from 2 Timothy — the first book the BUILD course examines. Read each passage carefully before answering the question below.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, <sup>17</sup>that the person dedicated to God may be capable and equipped for every good work.
<sup>15</sup>Make every effort to present yourself before God as a proven worker who does not need to be ashamed, teaching the message of truth accurately.
<sup>7</sup>Think about what I am saying and the Lord will give you understanding of all this.
<sup>2</sup>And entrust what you heard me say in the presence of many others as witnesses to faithful people who will be competent to teach others as well.
<sup>14</sup>You, however, must continue in the things you have learned and are confident about. You know who taught you

elements.question:
For each of the five BUILD words, explain the link between the word and its paired verse from 2 Timothy. What does each verse tell us about that value — and about the training of local church leaders?

B — Biblical (2 Tim 3:16-17)
U — Understanding (2 Tim 2:15)
I — In-service (2 Tim 2:7)
L — Leadership (2 Tim 2:2)
D — Development (2 Tim 3:14)
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through each letter in turn. For each one: what does the word mean, and how does the verse illuminate it?
elements.sampleAnswer:
B — Biblical (2 Tim 3:16-17): ‘Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness.’ The link is clear: BUILD is committed to learning that comes under the authority of God’s word. The verse tells us that Scripture is sufficient and powerful for every aspect of ministry formation — it is not one resource among many but the foundational one.

U — Understanding (2 Tim 2:15): ‘Teaching the message of truth accurately.’ The link is the call to handle Scripture with skill and integrity — not carelessly or dishonestly, but as a proven worker who has nothing to be ashamed of. Understanding the Bible well, and teaching it accurately, is a core commitment of BUILD.

I — In-service (2 Tim 2:7): ‘Think about what I am saying and the Lord will give you understanding of all this.’ The link is the word ’reflect’ — understanding comes as we think and pray in the midst of ministry, not apart from it. BUILD’s training is done in-service: in the context of real church life and real ministry, where reflection and action are woven together.

L — Leadership (2 Tim 2:2): ‘Entrust what you heard me say... to faithful people who will be competent to teach others as well.’ The link is the chain of faithful leaders passing on what they have received. Leadership in BUILD is not about status but about faithful stewardship — receiving, growing, and passing on. This verse captures BUILD’s core vision of multiplying leaders.

D — Development (2 Tim 3:14): ‘You must continue in the things you have learned.’ The link is perseverance — continuing, not stopping. Development is a lifelong process, not a one-time event. BUILD is committed to equipping leaders to be life-long learners who keep growing long after any particular course has ended.
elements.questionHint: For each letter, ask two questions: (1) What does this word (Biblical, Understanding, etc.) mean as a value for a training programme? (2) What does the paired verse add — what specific light does it shed on why this value matters for local church leaders?

elements.text: 1.4 The Five Challenges We Face as Leaders
The five words BUILD stands for also highlight five real challenges that local church leaders face. BUILD has been designed to help leaders meet these challenges head-on.

elements.eyebrow: Five Challenges for Local Church Leaders
elements.term: The challenges BUILD is designed to address
elements.question: What are the five main challenges facing local church leaders today?
elements.answer:
1. Biblical — The challenge of keeping the Bible central.
In a world full of competing voices and pressures, it is easy for the Bible to drift from the centre of church life and ministry to the margins.

2. Understanding — The challenge of explaining and applying the teaching of the Bible.
Many leaders want to teach the Bible faithfully but lack the tools and training to do so with confidence and accuracy.

3. In-service — The challenge of remaining committed to mission.
The demands of church life can crowd out active engagement with the community and the world. Leaders need to be equipped and encouraged to stay on mission.

4. Leadership — The challenge of a lack of faithful leaders who can train others.
Many churches are limited by a shortage of leaders who are both faithful to the gospel and capable of equipping others. Multiplication of leaders is urgent.

5. Development — The challenge of persevering with gospel ministry.
Ministry is hard and long. Many leaders start well but do not continue. BUILD is designed to equip leaders to persevere — and to keep growing throughout their ministry lives.

elements.question: In what ways do these five challenges affect you personally in your ministry? Which do you feel most acutely, and why?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Be honest about the pressures you face. Which of the five challenges is most real and most urgent in your own ministry context right now?
elements.sampleAnswer: Responses will be personal and contextual. The question is designed to move from the abstract (five challenges) to the concrete (my challenges, in my church, in my situation). A useful prompt: for each of the five challenges, give yourself a score from 1 (not a significant issue) to 5 (an urgent and pressing problem). Then reflect on the two or three highest scores — what is causing that challenge, and what would it look like to begin addressing it through the BUILD programme?
elements.questionHint: Think about a specific moment in the past month when you felt the pressure of one of these challenges. What was the situation? What made it difficult? Which of the five does it most clearly illustrate?

elements.question: How can you and others in your BUILD group begin to overcome these challenges together? What practical steps could you take?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what you can do together as a learning community — not just what you can do alone…
elements.sampleAnswer: The word ’together’ in the question is important — BUILD is a community of learning, not just individual study. Some practical steps might include: committing to pray for each other’s specific ministry challenges; agreeing to hold each other accountable to the habits of Bible reading and teaching preparation; practising together (sharing testimonies, giving and receiving feedback on gospel explanations); and identifying one area where each participant will intentionally apply something from the BUILD course in their church before the next session. The programme is most effective when what is learnt in the group is practised in the local church.
elements.questionHint: Think about the specific resources BUILD gives you: the community of learners, the curriculum, the trainer, and the local church context. How can each of these be used to address the challenges you identified in the previous question?

elements.text: 1.5 BUILD’s Vision, Mission and Values

elements.term: What BUILD is aiming for, how it plans to get there, and what it believes
elements.question: What is BUILD’s vision, mission and set of values?
elements.answer:
Vision — a picture of the future BUILD hopes to see:
’BUILD’s vision is to see a multitude of well-equipped leaders at the grassroots building healthy churches across Africa’s Great Lakes Region and beyond.’

Mission — how BUILD aims to achieve that vision:
’BUILD’s mission is to enable churches to train their own leaders with a practical understanding of the gospel, Scripture and theology.’

Values — the foundational convictions that motivate and guide BUILD:

1. Biblical — BUILD believes in learning under Scripture. The Bible and its gospel must be central to all training if personal development and local church growth is to be genuine.

2. Understanding — BUILD believes in learning with integrity. Skilful and godly use of the Bible and its gospel are essential for the development of true leaders.

3. In-service — BUILD believes in learning in context. Learning is best done in the midst of ministry and mission, in partnership with local churches.

4. Leadership — BUILD believes in learning and leadership. Leaders who understand the gospel deeply and invest in others are essential for healthy churches.

5. Development — BUILD believes in learning for life. While every training course ends, Christian discipleship does not. BUILD equips leaders to be life-long learners.

elements.question: Which of BUILD’s five values resonates most strongly with you right now, and why? Which do you find most challenging to embody in your own ministry?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Reflect on the five values — Biblical, Understanding, In-service, Leadership, Development. Which feels most natural to you, and which is hardest to live out?
elements.sampleAnswer: Responses will vary significantly. The question is designed to personalise BUILD’s values rather than leave them as abstract principles. A helpful prompt: think about which value, if you lived it more fully, would make the greatest difference to your ministry right now. That is often the one that is both most needed and most challenging. Notice also whether the value you find most natural is the one BUILD most needs from you — sometimes our strengths in one area can obscure weaknesses in another.
elements.questionHint: Try to be concrete: don’t just say which value you like most in theory, but which one you most consistently live out in practice — and which one you most consistently fail to live out. What would need to change for the hardest one to become more real in your ministry?

elements.text: 1.6 The Structure of a BUILD Programme

BUILD programmes look different in different places, but they all share the same underlying structure — designed to deliver training in partnership with local churches. A helpful way to picture a BUILD programme is as a healthy tree: deep roots, a strong trunk, shady branches, and fruit for people to enjoy.

elements.alt: The BUILD programme pictured as a healthy tree. The roots are labelled ’2. FOUNDATION — Formal training-of-trainers’. The trunk is labelled ’3. FOCUS — Non-formal, local BUILD training groups’. The branches and canopy are labelled ’4. FRUIT — Informal learning in churches and other outcomes’. Above the tree, sun and rain are labelled ’1. FORCE — Coordination and curriculum’.
elements.caption: A BUILD programme pictured as a healthy tree: Force, Foundation, Focus, and Fruit

elements.term: Force — Foundation — Focus — Fruit
elements.question: How does a BUILD programme work? What are its four key elements?
elements.answer:
1. FORCE — Coordination and curriculum
The force behind BUILD is its curriculum and coordination. Programme coordinators ensure the work is well-managed and well-resourced. The BUILD course is the key resource put into the hands of trainers to equip others. It continues to be developed and translated.

2. FOUNDATION — Formal training-of-trainers
The foundation of BUILD is its formal training-of-trainers. This produces grassroots educators for local BUILD groups — trainers who can use the BUILD course and its materials to equip others. This training is sometimes done in partnership with a college, training centre, or network of churches. Trainers begin training participants in local BUILD groups during the course itself, equipping themselves more effectively in the process.

3. FOCUS — Non-formal training of local church leaders
The focus of BUILD is the non-formal training of local church leaders in local BUILD groups. Participants are church leaders at the grassroots — BUILD’s primary target group: mature Christians, lay workers, and others who lead within local congregations. These groups are led by BUILD trainers or local trainers who emerge from within the groups themselves.

4. FRUIT — Informal training and other impacts
The fruit of BUILD is the informal training and wider impact that flows from the programme. This includes more effective preaching, teaching, pastoral care, and outreach. Lives, churches, and communities are transformed as the gospel is proclaimed more effectively through word and deed.

elements.question: Where do you fit in the BUILD programme structure — Force, Foundation, Focus, or Fruit? What is your role, and what does that mean for how you engage with the programme?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about your role — are you a participant in a local group (Focus), a trainer (Foundation), a coordinator (Force)? And what does that role ask of you?
elements.sampleAnswer: Most participants reading this unit are at element 3 — the FOCUS of BUILD: the non-formal training of local church leaders. That means they are at the heart of the programme. Their engagement matters not only for their own growth but for the Fruit that flows from it into their local churches and communities. Understanding where you sit in the structure helps you see both the value of what you are doing and your responsibility to take it seriously — because what is learnt here is meant to flow outward into real ministry.
elements.questionHint: If you are a participant in a local BUILD group, you are at element 3 — the FOCUS. But you are also meant to contribute to element 4 — the FRUIT. What would it look like for your participation in this programme to bear fruit in your local church over the coming months?

elements.text: 1.7 Introducing the BUILD Course

A course or curriculum is a pathway for learning — designed to take you from your current level of attitudes, skills, and knowledge to a new level that will help you build your church. The BUILD course has ten modules, each built around a Bible book, a group of Bible books, a theological theme, and a leadership issue.

elements.term: A pathway through the Bible and into ministry
elements.question: What are the ten modules of the BUILD curriculum?
elements.answer:
Module 1 — 2 Timothy and the Pastoral Epistles: Preaching the Gospel and Godly Leadership

Module 2 — Genesis and the Pentateuch: Knowing God’s Story and Leading by Faith

Module 3 — Mark and the Gospels: The Servant King and Servant Leadership

Module 4 — Nehemiah and the Historical Books: Building God’s People and Strategic Leadership

Module 5 — Acts and the New Testament Story: The Mission of the Church and Missional Leadership

Module 6 — Psalms and Poetry: Worshipping the King and Wholehearted Leadership

Module 7 — Ephesians and the Epistles: God’s Plan for the Church and Local Church Leadership

Module 8 — Proverbs and Wisdom: The Fear of the Lord and Practical Leadership

Module 9 — Isaiah and the Prophets: The Sovereign King and Prophetic Leadership

Module 10 — Revelation and Apocalyptic: The Victorious Christ and Leaders that Endure

Note: Not all BUILD courses cover all modules.

elements.term: Every module contains fifteen units of four kinds
elements.question: What are the four types of learning unit in the BUILD curriculum?
elements.answer:
1. Tools — for encountering God’s word in God’s world. These units give you the knowledge and skills you need for handling the Bible well.

2. Encounters — with God’s word in God’s world. These units put the tools to work as you study passages of Scripture — discovering their meaning and learning how to teach them to others.

3. Reflection — for wisdom. These units help you think carefully about different issues and equip you to lead others in that process. They develop your skills of theological reflection.

4. Action — as leaders in mission. These units provide practical training in areas such as preaching, pastoral care, and leadership — essential skills for local church leaders.

elements.question: Looking at the four types of learning unit — Tools, Encounters, Reflection, and Action — which do you think will be most stretching for you personally, and why?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about your own strengths and weaknesses as a learner and a leader. Which type of unit will push you hardest — and which do you most look forward to?
elements.sampleAnswer: Responses will vary. Some leaders are strong practitioners (Action) but less comfortable with careful Bible study (Tools and Encounters). Others are good readers and thinkers (Reflection) but find it harder to translate learning into practical ministry (Action). The four types are designed to complement each other: Tools and Encounters develop the foundation; Reflection develops wisdom; Action develops practice. The goal is not to be equally strong in all four, but to grow in each — and to recognise that all four are necessary for well-rounded gospel ministry.
elements.questionHint: Think about your previous experience of learning and ministry. Where do you feel most confident — studying the Bible, thinking through issues, or doing practical ministry? Which of the four unit types addresses an area where you know you need to grow?

elements.text: 1.8 Conclusion
1.8.1 The Word ’BUILD’ in the New Testament

elements.question: Can you think of other verses in the New Testament where the word ’build’ or the idea of building is used when talking about the life of the church? List as many as you can.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think across the New Testament — the Gospels, Paul’s letters, Peter’s letters. Where do you find building language used for the church and its growth?
elements.sampleAnswer: Building language is woven throughout the New Testament. Some key examples: 1 Corinthians 3:9-15 — Paul describes the church as ’God’s building’ and himself as a ’skilled master builder’ who lays the foundation (Christ), warning that others must be careful how they build on it. Ephesians 2:19-22 — the church is described as a ’holy temple’ being ’built together’ as a dwelling place for God by his Spirit, with Christ as the cornerstone and the apostles and prophets as the foundation. 1 Peter 2:4-5 — believers are ’living stones’ being built into a spiritual house. Romans 14:19 and 15:2 — Paul urges believers to pursue what builds up (edifies) one another. 1 Corinthians 14:26 — all things in the gathered church should be done ’for building up’. The Greek word oikodome (building up, edification) is one of Paul’s favourite ways of describing what Christian community and ministry should achieve.
elements.questionHint: Think about the letters of Paul — especially 1 Corinthians, Ephesians, and Romans. What images does he use for the church as a structure? Also think about 1 Peter. Where does building language appear, and who is doing the building in each case?

elements.question: What do these ’building’ verses teach us, and why is the word ’build’ so important in the New Testament? What does the building metaphor communicate about the nature of the church and the task of its leaders?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what the building metaphor implies — about foundations, materials, architects, and what a finished building is for…
elements.sampleAnswer: The building metaphor communicates several vital truths. First, the church is something being constructed — it is not yet finished, and it requires ongoing work. Second, foundations matter enormously: build on the wrong foundation and the whole structure is at risk. Christ is the only true foundation (1 Cor 3:11), which is why faithfulness to the gospel is not optional but structural. Third, every member is a living stone (1 Pet 2:5) — the whole community participates in the building, not just the leaders. Fourth, the goal of building is a dwelling place — a community where God’s presence is known and experienced. And fifth, leaders are builders who must be skilled and careful: they are accountable for what they build and how they build it. The metaphor is rich precisely because it holds together God’s initiative (he is building his church) and human responsibility (we are workers in his project).
elements.questionHint: Think about what a building requires: a sure foundation, quality materials, a clear design, skilled workers, and time. How does each of these elements map onto what is required for a healthy church? What happens when any one of them is missing?

elements.text: 1.8.2 A Commitment to Learning

elements.question: Don’t worry if you haven’t fully grasped all of this material yet — it will make more sense as you move through the training. But take a moment now to reflect: what is one specific commitment you want to make to the BUILD programme and to your own growth as a leader?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection — write it as a specific, personal commitment rather than a general intention…

elements.text: Close this unit in prayer. Remember the important phrase and promise in the BUILD anchor verse: ’I will build my church’ (Matthew 16:18). Thank the Lord Jesus that he has promised to build his church — that he will nurture and protect it until it is firm and steadfast forever, and that he does so in partnership with us. Ask him to make you a faithful and skilled builder in his great building project, and commit the BUILD programme — its trainers, its participants, its local churches — to his hands.

chapters.chapterTitle: How to Hear God’s Voice Today (Tools)

elements.text: In Unit 1 we learnt that the letter B in BUILD stands for Biblical — keeping the Bible central in life and ministry. The letter U stands for Understanding — using the Bible well. That discussion was grounded in 2 Timothy 2:15: the call to be a worker who correctly handles the word of truth. But why is understanding the Bible so important? To answer that, we need to ask a different question first: how does God speak today?

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Explain how someone can recognise God’s voice today
2. Illustrate why God’s speech in the past still speaks today
3. Point out the challenges we face in understanding the Bible
4. State the four steps for understanding a book of the Bible
5. Apply the four steps to a Bible verse
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.text: 2.1 The Importance of Understanding the Bible

elements.question: List the ways in which you and others think that God speaks to people today.
elements.answerPlaceholder: List as many ways as you can think of — through Scripture, prayer, other people, circumstances, creation, dreams, and anything else you have heard suggested…
elements.sampleAnswer: Common answers include: the Bible (Scripture), prayer, the Holy Spirit speaking to the heart, preaching and teaching, other Christians, circumstances and providence, creation, dreams and visions, prophecy, and the church community. Different Christian traditions emphasise different items on this list — some emphasising the immediacy of the Spirit’s voice, others the primacy of Scripture. The purpose of the question is to surface the range of views people hold before examining what the Bible itself says about how God speaks.
elements.questionHint: Don’t limit yourself to what you personally believe — include what you have heard others claim. The goal is to get a full picture of the range of views before we examine which sources can be trusted and how they relate to each other.

elements.question: Arrange the sources on your list in order of importance. Which do you place at the top, and why?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Number the sources from your list above — most important (1) to least important. Then explain why you placed the top one where you did…
elements.sampleAnswer: This exercise often reveals significant differences even within a single church or group. Some will place Scripture at the top because it is the only written, fixed, universally accessible form of God’s communication. Others may place direct spiritual experience higher because it feels more immediate and personal. The follow-up question — why did you place your top source there? — is the most important part. It begins to surface the assumptions and convictions people bring to the question of authority, which the rest of the unit will address from Romans 15:4.
elements.questionHint: Notice whether the sources you have ranked highly are ones that can be checked and tested — and by what standard they are tested. Which of your sources provides the benchmark by which the others are evaluated?

elements.text: 2.2 Romans 15:4 and Hearing God’s Voice Today

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>For everything that was written in former times was written for our instruction, so that through endurance and through encouragement of the scriptures we may have hope.

elements.question: What do you think Romans 15:4 tells us about God and about how he speaks today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Read the verse carefully. What does it assume about God? What does it claim about Scripture? What does it promise to those who engage with it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Romans 15:4 tells us several important things. First, it assumes that God is a speaking God — one who has communicated with his people through written words. Second, it asserts that those past words were written with a deliberate future purpose: ‘for our instruction.’ The Bible was not only for its original recipients; it was written for us too. Third, it identifies what Scripture produces in those who engage with it: endurance, encouragement, and hope. This is not merely academic knowledge — it is life-shaping formation. The verse implies that if we want endurance, encouragement, and hope, we must go to the Scriptures to find them.
elements.questionHint: Look at three things in the verse: (1) what God has done (‘everything that was written’), (2) for whom he did it (‘for our instruction’), and (3) what it produces (‘endurance, encouragement, hope’). What does each of those three things tell you about God and about the Bible?

elements.text: 2.2.1 The God Who Speaks Today

God is a speaking God — ‘And God said’ is one of the first things we read in the Bible (Genesis 1:3). We have begun to discuss the claim that he continues to speak in a range of ways today. But this immediately raises a question: how can we recognise God’s voice? How can we be confident we have truly heard his word? How do we discern between the Spirit and other spirits, between the ’word of truth’ and ’godless chatter’ (2 Tim 2:15-16)?
2.2.2 God Has Spoken in the Past

God spoke to his people in the past, and those words have been captured in the Bible. When Paul says ’everything that was written in former times’ in Romans 15:4, he is thinking of the Old Testament Scriptures — he has just quoted Psalm 69:9 in the previous verse (Romans 15:3). This is a solid, fixed foundation: God has spoken, and what he said has been reliably preserved. The New Testament has since been added to those writings, giving us the complete Christian Bible. For us today, the whole Bible ’was written in the past’ and ’was written for our instruction.’
2.2.3 God Speaks Today Through What He Has Said in the Past

But that leaves a gap between God’s words in the past and our lives today. How does God bridge that gap? Paul’s answer in Romans 15:4 is clear: ‘everything that was written in former times was written for our instruction.’ God speaks to us today through what he has spoken in the past. He teaches us as his written word is explained and applied — with the help of God’s Holy Spirit — to our lives, churches, and communities today.

elements.alt: Diagram illustrating how God speaks today through Scripture. At the top centre is a crown labelled ’The God who speaks’. A solid diagonal line (labelled 2) runs down-left to a box labelled ’God has spoken in the past — The Bible was made: everything that was written in the past’. A dotted diagonal line (labelled 1) runs down-right to a box labelled ’God speaks today’. A solid horizontal arrow (labelled 3) connects the past box to the today box, labelled: The Bible was made for us, it was written to teach us.
elements.caption: How God speaks today through what he has said in the past (Romans 15:4)

elements.text: 2.2.4 The Message of Romans 15:4 in Summary

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>But we who are strong ought to bear with the failings of the weak, and not to please ourselves. <sup>2</sup>Let each of us please his neighbour for his good, to build him up. <sup>3</sup>For even Christ did not please himself, but just as it is written, ‘The insults of those who insult you have fallen on me.’ <sup>4</sup>For everything that was written in former times was written for our instruction, so that through endurance and through encouragement of the scriptures we may have hope.

elements.question: Looking at Romans 15:1-4, why does Paul quote from the Old Testament (Psalm 69:9) in verse 3, and then explain his reason for doing so in verse 4? What does this tell us about how Paul expected Scripture to function in the life of the church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what Paul is trying to achieve in Romans 15:1-3. Why does he turn to the Old Testament — and what does his explanation in v. 4 reveal about the authority he gives to it?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul is encouraging the church in Rome to bear with one another and not to please themselves. To reinforce this, he turns to the example of Christ — and then immediately grounds that example in Old Testament Scripture (Psalm 69:9). This is significant: Paul does not treat the Old Testament as a history book or a cultural relic. He uses it as a living word that speaks directly to the situation of the Roman church. His explanation in v. 4 makes this principle explicit: the reason he can do this is that ’everything that was written in former times was written for our instruction.’ Paul models what he explains — he reads Scripture across the gap of time and applies it to a new situation. That is exactly what church leaders are called to do.
elements.questionHint: Notice the structure: Paul makes a practical appeal (v. 1-2), supports it with Christ’s example (v. 3a), grounds that example in Scripture (v. 3b), and then explains why he can do that (v. 4). What does this sequence reveal about how Paul thinks Scripture, Christ’s example, and the church’s life relate to each other?

elements.text: This means that if we are to hear God’s voice today, understanding the Bible is essential. The Bible is God’s way of communicating with his people today — and it is the measure by which all other words and claims are to be weighed and evaluated. The more we hear God’s word and absorb it, the more naturally we will recognise God’s voice and discern truth from error. It is like learning to recognise a friend’s voice on the phone after years of speaking with them face to face: familiarity builds confidence.

elements.question: In what practical ways can a church leader grow in their familiarity with Scripture so that they become better at recognising God’s voice and discerning truth from error?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about habits, disciplines, and community practices that deepen a person’s engagement with the Bible over time…
elements.sampleAnswer: Practical ways include: regular personal Bible reading — not just for sermon preparation but for personal nourishment; reading large portions of Scripture at a sitting to grasp the flow of whole books; memorising key passages; studying the Bible in community with others who will challenge and enrich your reading; sitting under good preaching and teaching; reading widely in biblical theology to understand how the parts of Scripture fit together; and praying with Scripture, allowing it to shape the content and direction of prayer. The goal is not merely intellectual familiarity but the kind of deep, lived engagement that makes God’s word instinctive — a default frame of reference for all of life.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between someone who reads the Bible only when preparing a talk, and someone who lives in it constantly. What would be different about how each person hears a new claim, evaluates a new idea, or responds to a pastoral crisis?

elements.text: 2.3 The Challenge of Understanding the Bible

We have seen that in order to hear God’s voice today we need to establish what he has said in the past. This is the foundational principle of the BUILD curriculum: we must discover what God has said to his people in the past before declaring it to his people today. But this principle creates both opportunities and difficulties.

elements.question: In what ways does the principle of ’discovering what God has said in the past’ make the job of understanding the Bible easier?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what it means to have a fixed, written text — what advantages does that give us in knowing what God has said?
elements.sampleAnswer: Having a written, fixed text makes understanding easier in several ways. The words are preserved and do not change — we are not dependent on oral tradition or memory. The same text is accessible to all people, in all places, at all times. Because the message has been captured in writing, it can be studied carefully, compared across books, and understood progressively. The fixed nature of Scripture also means that claims about what God has said can be tested and evaluated — anyone can check whether a teaching is faithful to the text. And because the whole Bible has a coherent storyline and consistent themes, the parts help us understand each other.
elements.questionHint: Think about what it would be like to have only an oral tradition — messages passed down by memory and word of mouth. What would be lost? What does the written, fixed nature of the Bible give us that we might otherwise not have?

elements.question: In what ways does the fact that God’s word was given in the past make the job of understanding the Bible harder?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the distance between us and the original readers — in language, culture, history, geography, and in the story of salvation…
elements.sampleAnswer: The gap between the original setting and ours creates real difficulties. The Bible was written in ancient languages (Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek) that most people do not read. It was written in cultures very different from our own — in a world without phones, electricity, or nation-states as we know them. The historical events and geographical places it references are unfamiliar to many readers. The literary forms it uses (ancient letters, poetry, prophecy, apocalyptic writing) follow conventions that are not always obvious to modern readers. And the Bible spans many centuries of redemptive history — understanding where a particular passage sits in that story requires knowledge of the whole. All of this means that understanding the Bible well requires genuine effort, humility, and help.
elements.questionHint: Think about the gaps a reader has to cross: language, culture, history, geography, and the story of salvation (where does this book fit in the Bible’s overall plot?). Which of these gaps do you personally find most challenging?

elements.question: In what ways is the situation of God’s people in the past similar to our situation today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about human nature, the nature of sin, the need for the gospel, the challenges of leadership and community — what has stayed the same across the centuries?
elements.sampleAnswer: Despite the surface differences, the similarities are profound and numerous. Human nature has not changed: the same pride, fear, doubt, grief, temptation, and longing for God that appear in the Psalms and the letters of Paul are present in every human being today. The gospel need is the same: every person in every culture is a sinner in need of a saviour. The church faces the same basic challenges: false teaching, leadership failure, internal conflict, and the pressure to conform to surrounding culture. And believers share the same Spirit, the same Scriptures, and the same hope — the return of Christ and the new creation. These deep similarities are what make the Bible’s word so immediately applicable across the centuries.
elements.questionHint: Think about a specific struggle or temptation you face — or that your church faces — and ask: did the people of God in the Bible ever face something similar? Where do you see your own situation reflected in the pages of Scripture?

elements.question: In what ways is the situation of God’s people in the past different from our situation today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the differences in history, culture, the stage of God’s redemptive plan, and the completeness of revelation we now have…
elements.sampleAnswer: There are significant differences too. The most important is our place in the story of salvation: Old Testament believers were looking forward to Christ’s coming, while we look back on his completed work of redemption. We have the full New Testament, the completed canon of Scripture — they did not. The specific cultural, political, and social settings of the biblical world differ greatly from ours: the Roman Empire, the Jerusalem temple, the Old Testament sacrificial system, and the social structures of the ancient Near East are not our context. Some of God’s commands in Scripture were specific to a particular people, time, or phase of his redemptive plan — and wise interpretation requires us to understand that. This is why understanding where a passage sits in the Bible’s overall story is so important.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between an Old Testament believer and a New Testament believer, and between a first-century Christian and a Christian today. What has changed — in terms of what God has done, what has been revealed, and what we are waiting for?

elements.text: 2.4 The Process of Understanding the Bible

We can use a step-by-step approach to understand any book of the Bible. The four steps below form a framework that will be used throughout the BUILD curriculum. Think of the gap between the biblical world and today as a river to cross — with the world of the Bible on one side and today’s world on the other. As church leaders we need to have one foot in the world of the Bible and one foot in the world today: to understand both sides and to build a bridge.

elements.eyebrow: Four Steps for Understanding a Bible Book
elements.term: The framework used throughout the BUILD curriculum
elements.question: What are the four steps for understanding any book of the Bible?
elements.answer:
Step 1: Establish the original CONTEXT of the book
What was going on when this book was written? Understand the situation of the original hearers — the ’old home’ of the text — while keeping in mind our own context, the ’new home’ where God’s voice will be heard today. The gap between these two contexts is created by differences in culture, language, history, geography, and where the book sits in the story of salvation.

Step 2: Discover the CONTENT of the book
In the light of that context, what is the main point the author is making to his original hearers? What main message did the book carry in its original setting? What important themes does it communicate?

Step 3: Make the CONNECTIONS with our context
Once we have begun to discover the content, we build the bridge — we make connections between their situation and ours today. This includes both the differences (which we must navigate carefully) and the similarities (which often make the connection more direct).

Step 4: Teach the CONTENT of the book today
Finally, we teach the relevant content of the book today — making the connections between then and now for the people we serve.

elements.alt: Diagram of the four steps for understanding a Bible book. On the left is a box labelled ’1. Establish the original CONTEXT of a Bible book written in the past’. In the centre is a box labelled ’2. Discover the CONTENT of the book’ sitting above another box labelled ’3. Make the CONNECTIONS — Gap: culture, language, history, place, salvation’, with arrows pointing left and right. On the right is a box labelled ’Keep in mind the new CONTEXT for understanding the book today’, above which is a box labelled ’4. Teach the CONTENT of the book’. Arrows connect the steps from left to right across the gap.
elements.caption: The four steps for understanding a Bible book — bridging the gap between the biblical world and today

elements.question: In your own words, explain what the ’gap’ between the biblical world and our world today consists of. What are the main components of that gap, and why does it matter that we take them seriously?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the different kinds of distance between us and the original readers — cultural, linguistic, historical, geographical, and theological…
elements.sampleAnswer: The gap has several distinct components. Cultural distance: the assumptions, values, social structures, and everyday practices of the biblical world are very different from ours. Linguistic distance: the Bible was written in ancient Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek, and even good translations cannot fully capture every nuance. Historical distance: the events, empires, and institutions of the biblical world require background knowledge to understand. Geographical distance: the places named in Scripture are real but unfamiliar, and their significance often requires explanation. And most importantly, there is a salvation-historical distance: where does this book sit in the unfolding story of God’s redemption — before or after the cross? Each of these gaps must be taken seriously if we are to apply Scripture faithfully rather than carelessly.
elements.questionHint: Imagine explaining to a new Christian why they cannot simply read a command given to Israel in Leviticus and apply it directly today, or why Paul’s instruction about a specific person in a first-century letter still has something to say to a twenty-first-century church. What would you need to explain about the gap?

elements.text: 2.5 Illustrating the Four Steps: 2 Timothy 4:1
We will apply the four steps to 1 and 2 Timothy in Unit 3. But we can illustrate them now using a single phrase. In 2 Timothy 4:1 Paul says to Timothy: ’I give you this charge.’

elements.term: What does the word ’charge’ mean in 2 Timothy 4:1?
elements.question:
The word ’charge’ can mean different things in different contexts. Which meaning does Paul intend?

A. An explosive device
B. A bill or invoice to be paid
C. A duty to be carried out
elements.answer:
The answer is C — a duty to be carried out. The context of the phrase confirms this immediately:

’In the presence of God and of Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead, and in view of his appearing and his kingdom, I give you this charge: preach the word…’ (2 Tim 4:1-2a, NET)

The charge Paul gives Timothy is the solemn duty to proclaim the message of the gospel. The context — the presence of God, the judgement of Christ, his appearing and kingdom — gives the word enormous weight. Understanding the context has unlocked the meaning of the words.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>I solemnly charge you before God and Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead, and by his appearing and his kingdom: <sup>2</sup>preach the message, be ready whether it is convenient or not, reprove, rebuke, exhort with complete patience and instruction.

elements.question: STEP 2 — CONTENT: What is the main content of 2 Timothy 4:1-2? What command has Timothy been given, and what gives that command its weight?
elements.answerPlaceholder: What is Timothy being told to do? And what does the surrounding context — the reference to God, Christ, judgement, and the kingdom — add to the force of the command?
elements.sampleAnswer: The content is clear and weighty. Timothy has been given a command: to proclaim the message of the gospel — to preach the word, whether it is convenient or not, reproving, rebuking, and exhorting with complete patience and instruction. The command is given enormous weight by its context: it is issued in the presence of God and Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead, and in view of his appearing and his kingdom. This is not a casual request from a colleague — it is a solemn charge issued under the authority and in the sight of the eternal judge and king. The content (preach the word) cannot be separated from the context that gives it its gravity.
elements.questionHint: Notice what Paul says before the command. Why does he invoke the presence of God, the judgement of Christ, and the kingdom? What does that framework add to the force of the instruction ’preach the word’?

elements.question: STEP 3 — CONNECTIONS: What connections can you make between the charge given to Timothy in 2 Timothy 4:1-2 and the situation of church leaders today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about who Paul is addressing, what the similarities are between Timothy’s situation and yours, and what the same charge means for leaders in your context today…
elements.sampleAnswer: The connections are direct and powerful. The charge Paul gives Timothy was given to a church leader facing a specific crisis — but the underlying commission is universal. All believers are called to witness to Christ; those called to lead God’s church have a particular and solemn responsibility to proclaim the gospel. The context that gave the charge its weight for Timothy gives it the same weight today: Christ Jesus will still judge the living and the dead; his appearing and his kingdom are still coming. The urgency has not diminished. The need continues. And so the bridge between then and now is easily built — the charge still stands, and its authority is unchanged.
elements.questionHint: What has changed between Timothy’s situation and yours — and what has stayed the same? Focus especially on the framework Paul uses: the judgement, the appearing, the kingdom. Are these still future realities for us today?

elements.question: STEP 4 — TEACHING: How would you use 2 Timothy 4:1-2 to teach your own church or community about their responsibility to proclaim the gospel? What main point would you make, and how would you raise the issue with your listeners?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about your specific context — your church, your community, the people you lead. How would you bring this passage to bear on them, and what response would you hope to evoke?
elements.sampleAnswer: A teaching from 2 Tim 4:1-2 might open with the question: ‘What would it feel like to receive a personal charge directly from God — witnessed by Christ himself, who will one day judge everything you have done?’ That framing captures the solemnity of the passage. The main point could be: because Christ is coming to judge and to establish his kingdom, proclaiming the gospel is not optional — it is the church’s primary, urgent, non-negotiable task. Application might include: assessing whether the church’s preaching is genuinely gospel-centred; challenging individuals about their personal witness; and examining whether the church treats gospel proclamation as urgent or as one activity among many. Leaders have a particular responsibility here — but the charge to point to Christ belongs to the whole community.
elements.questionHint: Think about the specific resistances or distractions your listeners face — what makes them reluctant to proclaim the gospel? How would the framing of 2 Tim 4:1 (the presence of God, the coming judgement) speak to those specific resistances?

elements.text: 2.6 Conclusion

We are following in Timothy’s footsteps. He was called to be ’a worker who does not need to be ashamed and who correctly handles the word of truth’ (2 Tim 2:15). Correctly handling the message of the gospel means first grasping the importance of understanding the Bible. It is important because God has chosen to speak to his people today through the Scriptures he gave in the past. The four steps we have introduced in this unit give us a framework for doing that work faithfully and well — and we will apply them to 1 and 2 Timothy in Unit 3.

elements.question: Looking back over this unit, what is the most important thing you are taking away about how God speaks today and why understanding the Bible is essential?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Which of the four gaps between the biblical world and our own (cultural, linguistic, historical, geographical, salvation-historical) do you find most challenging in your own Bible reading and teaching? What would help you to bridge it more effectively?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text:
Close this unit in prayer — using Romans 15:4 itself as your guide. Ask God that you and all his people would learn and benefit from the endurance and encouragement of the Scriptures, and find in them the gospel hope they promise:

’For everything that was written in former times was written for our instruction, so that through endurance and through encouragement of the scriptures we may have hope.’ (Romans 15:4)

In Unit 2 we learnt four steps for understanding a Bible book. In this unit we unpack those steps in detail and apply them to 1 Timothy — giving us a tool we can use to understand any Bible book. Towards the end of the unit we will also look briefly at 2 Timothy by comparing and contrasting it with 1 Timothy.

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Present the small, practical steps for understanding a book of the Bible
2. Use a set of questions to discover the setting of a Bible book
3. List the main themes of a Bible book
4. Summarise the situation and message of 1 Timothy
5. Compare and contrast 1 and 2 Timothy
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.term: Four steps for understanding any Bible book
elements.question:
Step 1: Establish the original CONTEXT of the book
Step 2: Discover the CONTENT of the book
Step 3: Make the CONNECTIONS with our context
Step 4: Teach the CONTENT of the book today
elements.answer:
Step 1 — Context breaks down into three questions: Where does the book fit in the Bible’s story? What situation was it written for? What style of writing is it?

Step 2 — Content asks: What is the structure? What are the main themes? What is the message and purpose?

Step 3 — Connections asks: What links are there between their situation and ours today?

Step 4 — Teaching asks: What main points would you make? How would you raise these issues with your listeners?

elements.text: 3.1 Step 1: Establishing the Original Context
Establishing the original context involves three aspects. Remember these three words: story, situation, and style.
3.1.1 Story

The word ’story’ is shorthand for ’the story of the Bible as a whole’. The most important question to ask first is: where does this book fit in the Bible’s overall plot? In particular, was it written before or after Jesus Christ came into the world? This shapes everything about how we read and apply it.

Consider these questions as you place any book on the Bible’s timeline:
  • Is it in the Old Testament or New Testament?
  • Is it about God’s promises or the fulfilment of those promises?
  • Is it looking forward to the work of Christ, or looking back to what he has done?
  • Is it expecting the cross and resurrection, or explaining it?

elements.alt: Diagram showing the cross at the centre of the Bible’s timeline: Old Testament (Promise, Looking forward, Expectation) on the left; New Testament (Fulfilment, Looking back, Explanation) on the right.
elements.caption: The cross as the hinge of the Bible’s story

elements.text:
1 Timothy was written after Christ’s coming. It is a New Testament letter written in a time of fulfilment — it looks back to the completed work of Christ, explains the gospel and its implications for the churches, and handles the Old Testament promises in the light of what Christ has done.

This also helps us make connections with our own situation today. We too are in the last days, waiting for Christ’s return. We share the same Spirit and the same gospel. That is why these letters are so directly applicable to us.

elements.question: In your own words, why does it matter whether a Bible book was written before or after Christ came?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about how the ’promise / fulfilment’ distinction affects the way we read and apply a passage…
elements.sampleAnswer: It matters because a passage written before Christ came is looking forward in hope and expectation to what God will do. A passage written after Christ came is looking back and explaining what God has done. The first group requires us to ask ’how does Christ fulfil this?’ while the second group can be applied more directly to those of us who also live on this side of the cross. Getting this right stops us from misreading the Old Testament as if Christ had not yet come, or misreading the New Testament as if the cross were still in the future.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between reading a letter written before a wedding and one written after it. The tone, focus, and way you apply the contents would be quite different.

elements.text: 3.1.2 Situation

It is very important to discover the situation that the book was written for — its historical setting. Understanding this brings the words and phrases to life. But where do we find that information?

The good news is that the most important resource for understanding the Bible is the Bible itself. This is based on the doctrine of the ’sufficiency of Scripture’: the Bible provides all the information we need to understand what it says about our salvation.

elements.eyebrow: Three places to find background information — within the Bible
elements.term: The Bible interprets the Bible
elements.question: Where should you look when you want to understand the setting of a Bible letter?
elements.answer:
1. Other parts of the Bible shed light on the setting of a book. The New Testament repeatedly refers to the Old Testament (e.g. 1 Timothy 5:18 quotes Deuteronomy 25:4 and Luke 10:7). The book of Acts often contains important background for understanding the epistles.

2. Related Bible books provide useful information too. Reading the other Pastoral Epistles — 2 Timothy and Titus — gives insights into the situation reflected in 1 Timothy.

3. The Bible book itself provides the information we need. We can read 1 Timothy carefully and discover its own situation by asking the right questions.

elements.text: To discover the setting of a letter, you need to put on a detective’s hat. Ask five key questions — Who? Where? What? Why? When? — and look for evidence in the text itself.
Applying the Five Questions to 1 Timothy
Read 1 Timothy at some point to familiarise yourself with the whole book. For now we will focus on the beginning of the letter and the key passages below.

elements.passageText:
1From Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by the command of God our Savior and of Christ Jesus our hope, 2to Timothy, my genuine child in the faith. Grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.
3As I urged you when I was leaving for Macedonia, stay on in Ephesus to instruct certain people not to spread false teachings, 4nor to occupy themselves with myths and endless genealogies. Such things promote useless speculation rather than God’s redemptive plan that operates through faith. 5But the goal of our instruction is love that comes from a pure heart, a good conscience, and a sincere faith. 6Some have strayed from these and turned away to empty discussion. 7They want to be teachers of the law, but they do not understand what they are saying or the things they insist on so confidently.

<sup>18</sup>I am giving you this instruction, Timothy my child, in keeping with the prophecies once spoken about you, in order that with such encouragement you may fight the good fight. <sup>19</sup>To do this you must hold firmly to faith and a good conscience, which some have rejected and so have suffered shipwreck in regard to the faith. <sup>20</sup>Among these are Hymenaeus and Alexander, whom I handed over to Satan to be taught not to blaspheme.

elements.question: WHO is addressing whom in 1 Timothy? Which different people and groups are involved?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at 1 Tim 1:1-7 and 1:18-20. Also read 1 Tim 4:6. Who is writing? Who is receiving it? Who else is mentioned?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul is writing (1:1) — describing himself as an apostle of Christ Jesus. He is writing to Timothy, whom he calls ’my genuine child in the faith’ (1:2) and ’Timothy my child’ (1:18). Timothy appears to be Paul’s trusted co-worker who has been left in Ephesus. The letter also mentions ’certain people’ who are spreading false teachings (1:3), and names two individuals — Hymenaeus and Alexander — who have rejected faith and a good conscience (1:20). In 4:6, Paul calls Timothy ’a good servant of Christ Jesus’, suggesting Timothy is a church leader or pastor in Ephesus.
elements.questionHint: Count the different named individuals and unnamed groups. Who is Paul worried about, and who is he encouraging?

elements.question: WHERE is the letter being written to, and are there any clues about where Paul is writing from?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at 1 Tim 1:3 for the destination. Are there any indications of where Paul might be?
elements.sampleAnswer: The letter is being written to Ephesus — Paul tells Timothy to ’stay on in Ephesus’ (1:3). This is confirmed when we look at Acts, which records Paul’s significant ministry in Ephesus (Acts 19–20) and his farewell to the Ephesian elders. Paul mentions that he is ’leaving for Macedonia’ (1:3), so he is probably writing from Macedonia or en route there. This matches what we know of Paul’s movements after his release from his first Roman imprisonment.
elements.questionHint: 1 Timothy 1:3 mentions two places. Which one is Timothy staying in, and which one is Paul heading to?

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>I hope to come to you soon, but I am writing these things to you in case I am delayed, <sup>15</sup>so that you will know how people ought to conduct themselves in the household of God, because it is the church of the living God, the support and bulwark of the truth. <sup>16</sup>And beyond all question, the mystery of godliness is great: He was revealed in the flesh, vindicated by the Spirit, seen by angels, proclaimed among Gentiles, believed on in the world, taken up in glory.

elements.question: WHAT is the main message of 1 Timothy? What key themes emerge from 1:3-20 and 3:1-16?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at 1 Tim 1:3-20 and 3:1-16. What subjects does Paul keep coming back to?
elements.sampleAnswer: Several key themes emerge: (1) The danger of false teaching — Paul is deeply concerned about ’certain people’ spreading myths, endless genealogies, and teaching contrary to the gospel (1:3-7). (2) The importance of sound doctrine and the gospel — Paul repeatedly contrasts false teaching with ’the trustworthy message’ (1:15) and ’the mystery of godliness’ (3:16). (3) Godly conduct in the church — chapter 3 focuses on the qualifications for overseers and deacons, showing that how the church lives matters enormously. (4) The church as ’the household of God’ and ’the support and bulwark of the truth’ (3:15) — guarding the truth is the church’s calling.
elements.questionHint: Look for the things Paul seems most worried about (false teaching) and the things he most wants Timothy to protect and promote (sound doctrine, godly conduct).

elements.question: WHY is Paul writing 1 Timothy? What is the purpose of the letter?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look especially at 1 Tim 1:3 and 3:14-15. What does Paul say he is trying to achieve?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s purpose is twofold. First, negatively: to stop the spread of false teaching in Ephesus (1:3 — ‘instruct certain people not to spread false teachings’). Second, positively: so that Timothy and the church will know ’how people ought to conduct themselves in the household of God’ (3:15). Paul is writing a kind of ’church manual’ — not primarily about organisational structure, but about how the gospel shapes the life of a Christian community. He wants the church to be a faithful guardian of the truth (3:15).
elements.questionHint: Does Paul give a single clear purpose statement? Look at 3:15 — he almost gives you the purpose of the letter in one sentence.

elements.question: WHEN was 1 Timothy written? Where does it fit in the story of the New Testament and in relation to the other Pastoral Epistles?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about where 1 Timothy sits in relation to Paul’s life and letters, and in relation to 2 Timothy and Titus…
elements.sampleAnswer: 1 Timothy was most likely written after Paul’s release from his first Roman imprisonment (Acts 28), during a period of renewed missionary activity not recorded in Acts. This places it late in Paul’s life and ministry. It was probably written before 2 Timothy, which reflects a more desperate situation (Paul in prison again, expecting death). Together with Titus, these three letters are known as the Pastoral Epistles. What matters most is not the precise date but recognising that 1 Timothy is a post-resurrection, post-Pentecost letter written in the last days — the same era we live in today.
elements.questionHint: You don’t need an exact date. Focus on: Is it Old or New Testament? Before or after the cross? How does it relate to 2 Timothy — which letter feels more urgent?

elements.text: 3.1.3 Style

Different Bible books are written in different styles or genres (types of writing). A letter follows different rules to a poem; a story follows different rules to a proverb. 1 Timothy is a letter — an epistle. Letters are always written from someone, to someone, with a message, and for a purpose. They are also occasional: written on a particular occasion for a particular reason, which is why understanding the situation is so important.

It is also worth asking: how was it written? What is the tone and feel? Is Paul concerned, urgent, tender, firm? These emotional cues shape how we read the content.

elements.question: What is the tone and feel of 1 Timothy 1:1-20? Is Paul concerned, angry, encouraged, urgent — or a mixture? What does this tell you about how seriously he takes the situation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Read 1 Tim 1:1-20 and pay attention to the emotional temperature of the letter…
elements.sampleAnswer: The tone is a mixture of deep concern, warmth, and urgency. Paul is clearly worried about the false teachers and their damaging influence (1:3-7, 18-20) — the language of ’fight the good fight’ (1:18) and ’shipwreck in regard to the faith’ (1:19) signals real danger. At the same time, his tone towards Timothy is warm and fatherly — ‘Timothy my child’ (1:18). He also breaks into a doxology of praise to God (1:17), showing that even in a difficult situation, his heart is full of worship. The overall feel is urgent but not panicked — Paul is serious about the crisis, but confident in the gospel.
elements.questionHint: Notice the different registers: Paul’s language about the false teachers, his language to Timothy personally, and his spontaneous burst of praise in 1:17. What do these different tones reveal?

elements.text: 3.2 Step 2: Discovering the Content of 1 Timothy

Having established the context, we now look more carefully at the content of 1 Timothy. Discovering the general content of a book before looking at individual texts is important — like surveying a landscape before examining individual features. If you were preaching through the book, this survey would yield fruit for a whole series of sermons.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>This saying is trustworthy: ‘If someone aspires to the office of overseer, he desires a good work.’ <sup>2</sup>The overseer then must be above reproach, the husband of one wife, temperate, self-controlled, respectable, hospitable, an able teacher, <sup>3</sup>not a drunkard, not violent, but gentle, not contentious, free from the love of money. <sup>4</sup>He must manage his own household well and keep his children in control without losing his dignity. <sup>5</sup>But if someone does not know how to manage his own household, how will he care for the church of God? <sup>6</sup>He must not be a recent convert or he may become arrogant and fall into the punishment that the devil experienced. <sup>7</sup>And he must be well thought of by those outside the faith, so that he may not fall into disgrace and the trap of the devil.

elements.question: Based on what you have seen so far in 1 Timothy, what is the book mainly about? List the main things Paul is saying to Timothy and the church.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the themes running through the passages you have read — what does Paul keep coming back to?
elements.sampleAnswer: The main themes of 1 Timothy include: (1) Guarding against false teaching — this is the presenting crisis and runs through the whole letter. (2) The gospel — Paul is passionate about ’the glorious gospel of the blessed God’ (1:11) and the ’mystery of godliness’ (3:16). (3) Prayer and worship — chapter 2 calls the church to pray for all people and for those in authority. (4) Godly leadership — chapter 3 sets out the qualifications for overseers and deacons, focusing on character and sound doctrine. (5) How to live as the household of God — the church’s conduct must reflect its identity as ’the support and bulwark of the truth’ (3:15).
elements.questionHint: Try to organise your answer under 3-5 headings rather than listing every detail. What are the big themes rather than individual points?

elements.text: 3.3 Step 3: Making the Connections with Our Context
