We have already begun to make connections by seeing where 1 Timothy fits in the story of the Bible. We can build on that by noting the similarities and differences between their situation and ours.

elements.question: What similarities and connections do you see between the situation in 1 Timothy and your own situation, congregation, or the world you live and work in?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about false teaching, the need for godly leadership, prayer for those in authority, guarding the gospel…
elements.sampleAnswer: There are many direct connections. False teaching is not a first-century problem alone — every church today faces pressure from ideas that distort the gospel, whether from inside or outside. The need for godly, qualified leadership is just as urgent today as it was in Ephesus. Paul’s call to pray for those in authority (2:1-2) speaks directly to every Christian community that lives under government. The vision of the church as ’the household of God’ and ’the bulwark of the truth’ gives every local church today a clear identity and a serious responsibility.
elements.questionHint: Think about the pressures your own church or Christian community faces. Do any of them rhyme with what Paul is addressing in Ephesus?

elements.text: 3.4 Step 4: Teaching the Content of 1 Timothy Today
We will explore this step further in Unit 13. But begin to think now about how you might teach some of the content of 1 Timothy in your own context.

elements.question: What main points might you make if you were to preach or teach from 1 Timothy? How might you raise these issues with your listeners?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what your congregation most needs to hear from 1 Timothy, and how you would introduce the big themes…
elements.sampleAnswer: A series through 1 Timothy could open with the urgency of guarding sound doctrine against false teaching — showing that this is not optional but the church’s God-given calling. Subsequent talks could address the character of Christian leaders, the priority of prayer for the world, and how the gospel shapes every aspect of church life. The key is to help listeners see that 1 Timothy is not a dry church manual but a passionate letter from a father-figure to a son in the faith, driven by love for the gospel and for God’s people.
elements.questionHint: Start with the theme you think is most urgent for your own church context. What does your congregation most need to hear from this letter right now?

elements.text: 3.5 Understanding 2 Timothy

Our understanding of 1 Timothy helps us understand 2 Timothy. The two letters have a close relationship — like two brothers. We will apply Steps 1 and 2 to 2 Timothy, comparing and contrasting it with 1 Timothy. If you have time, read through 2 Timothy as a whole. If time is limited, focus on the specific verses referenced below.
Context: What does 2 Timothy share with 1 Timothy?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>From Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, to further the promise of life in Christ Jesus, <sup>2</sup>to Timothy, my dear child. Grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.

<sup>17</sup>And their teaching will spread like gangrene. Hymenaeus and Philetus are in this group. <sup>18</sup>They have strayed from the truth by saying that the resurrection has already occurred, and they are undermining some people’s faith.

elements.question: What similarities does 2 Timothy share with 1 Timothy in terms of context? Look at 2 Tim 1:1-2 and 2:17-18, and note any other parallels you observe.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Compare who is writing, who is receiving it, and what problems are present in both letters…
elements.sampleAnswer: Both letters share the same sender (Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus) and the same recipient (Timothy, Paul’s ’child in the faith’). Both open with the same grace greeting. Both letters also deal with false teachers: Hymenaeus appears in both (1 Tim 1:20 and 2 Tim 2:17), and the problem of teaching that undermines sound doctrine runs through both letters. The concern for Timothy as a pastor-leader guarding the truth is central to both.
elements.questionHint: Start with the opening verses — who writes, who receives, what greeting is given? Then look at 2:17-18 — does any name ring a bell from 1 Timothy?

elements.text: Context: How does 2 Timothy differ from 1 Timothy?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>May the Lord grant mercy to the family of Onesiphorus, because he often refreshed me and was not ashamed of my imprisonment. <sup>17</sup>But when he arrived in Rome, he eagerly searched for me and found me.

<sup>6</sup>For I am already being poured out as an offering, and the time for me to depart is at hand. <sup>7</sup>I have competed well; I have finished the race; I have kept the faith! <sup>8</sup>Finally the crown of righteousness is reserved for me. The Lord, the righteous Judge, will award it to me in that day — and not to me only, but also to all who have set their affection on his appearing.

elements.question: What differences does 2 Timothy have compared to 1 Timothy in terms of context? Look at 2 Tim 1:16-17 and 4:6-8, and note what has changed in Paul’s situation.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about where Paul is, what his circumstances are, and how the tone has shifted compared to 1 Timothy…
elements.sampleAnswer: The differences are significant. In 2 Timothy, Paul is imprisoned in Rome (1:16-17) — and this time it seems much more serious than his first imprisonment. He is clearly anticipating his own death: ‘the time for me to depart is at hand’ (4:6). The tone is more urgent and tender — this reads like a final letter from a father to a son. While 1 Timothy gives instructions for ongoing church life, 2 Timothy is a last will and testament, a charge to Timothy to carry on the gospel work after Paul is gone. The isolation is also more acute — ‘everyone in the province of Asia has deserted me’ (1:15).
elements.questionHint: What does ’poured out as an offering’ (4:6) suggest about Paul’s situation? Compare that to the relatively open and active ministry implied in 1 Timothy.

elements.text: Content: Similarities between 1 and 2 Timothy

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>Because of this I remind you to rekindle God’s gift that you possess through the laying on of my hands.

<sup>1</sup>So you, my child, be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus. <sup>2</sup>And what you have heard from me through many witnesses, entrust to faithful people who will be competent to teach others as well.

elements.question: What themes does 2 Timothy share with 1 Timothy in terms of content? Look at 2 Tim 1:6 and 2:1-2, and note the priorities that appear in both letters.
elements.answerPlaceholder: What does Paul keep emphasising in both letters? Think about leadership, the gospel, and faithfulness…
elements.sampleAnswer: Both letters share a deep concern for faithful leadership and the passing on of sound teaching. In 2 Tim 1:6 Paul urges Timothy to ’rekindle’ his God-given gift — a concern for Timothy’s personal faithfulness that echoes 1 Tim 4:14-16. In 2:1-2, Paul’s concern for the transmission of the gospel to ’faithful people who will be competent to teach others’ is a direct parallel to 1 Timothy’s vision of a church that guards and teaches the truth. The gospel itself — what has been ’entrusted’ — is central to both letters.
elements.questionHint: What is the ’good deposit’ or entrusted truth that Paul keeps coming back to in both letters? Why does its faithful transmission matter so much to him?

elements.text: Content: Differences between 1 and 2 Timothy

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>Hold to the standard of sound words that you heard from me and do so with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus. <sup>14</sup>Protect that good thing entrusted to you, through the Holy Spirit who lives within us.
<sup>12</sup>Now in fact all who want to live godly lives in Christ Jesus will be persecuted.

elements.question: What differences are there between 1 and 2 Timothy in terms of content? Look at 2 Tim 1:13-14 and 3:12, and note what is emphasised differently in 2 Timothy.
elements.answerPlaceholder: What concerns or themes appear in 2 Timothy that feel different in tone or emphasis from 1 Timothy?
elements.sampleAnswer: While 1 Timothy focuses on organising church life and countering false teaching in a relatively stable situation, 2 Timothy is more urgent and personal. The emphasis on suffering and persecution is much stronger in 2 Timothy — ‘all who want to live godly lives in Christ Jesus will be persecuted’ (3:12) and Paul speaks of his own chains and suffering repeatedly. The call to ’guard the good deposit’ (1:13-14) is more intense — it is no longer just about correcting teachers in Ephesus, but about surviving a worsening crisis without Paul present. 2 Timothy also has a stronger personal and emotional register — Paul’s loneliness and longing for Timothy’s presence (4:9, 21) are unique to this letter.
elements.questionHint: What word appears in 2 Timothy that you don’t hear as much in 1 Timothy? Think about what Paul expects faithful Christians to experience.

elements.text: 3.6 Conclusion

We have applied the four steps of understanding the Bible to 1 and 2 Timothy. We have discovered that in a situation where false teachers are damaging the church, these letters focus on protecting God’s people and the need for true teachers of God’s message — the gospel. This message becomes more urgent in 2 Timothy as Paul faces the end of his life and ministry.

Traditionally these three letters (1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, and Titus) have been called ’The Pastoral Epistles’. But that title has sometimes led to a narrow reading — as if they are mainly about church organisation and structure.

elements.question: How has this unit challenged the traditional understanding of 1 and 2 Timothy as primarily letters about church organisation? What is their deeper concern?
elements.answerPlaceholder: What have you discovered that goes beyond ’church management’ or ’leadership structure’?
elements.sampleAnswer: The unit shows that 1 and 2 Timothy are not primarily organisational handbooks. They are urgent, gospel-driven letters written in a crisis. The structures Paul discusses (overseers, deacons, etc.) are in service of a deeper goal: protecting the gospel and building a community that lives by it and passes it on. The real heart of the letters is the gospel itself — ‘the mystery of godliness’ (1 Tim 3:16) and ’the good deposit’ (2 Tim 1:14). Church organisation is a means to that end, not an end in itself.
elements.questionHint: Think about what Paul seems most passionate about in these letters. Is it getting the structures right — or is it something deeper that the structures are meant to serve?

elements.question: What alternative title to ’The Pastoral Epistles’ might better capture the true purpose of 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, and Titus?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what these three letters are really about — what one phrase captures their shared purpose?
elements.sampleAnswer: Several alternatives suggest themselves: ‘The Gospel Epistles’ (since they are fundamentally about protecting and proclaiming the gospel); ’The Church and the Gospel Letters’ (since they address how gospel communities should live and lead); or ’Letters for Gospel Workers’ (since they equip specific leaders for gospel ministry in difficult situations). The key is that any title should reflect the gospel-urgency of the letters rather than just their concern for church order.
elements.questionHint: What single word or phrase captures what Paul cares most about in these letters? Try to build a title around that word or phrase.

elements.text: Reflection Questions

elements.question: Which of the four steps for understanding a Bible book do you find most natural, and which do you find most challenging? Why?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: Has studying the context of 1 Timothy changed how you read any particular passage in it? If so, which one and how?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: What is one thing from 1 or 2 Timothy that you want to take away and apply in your own ministry or church this week?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: In this unit we have developed and applied a set of tools for understanding any book of the Bible. By asking where a book fits in the Bible’s story, what situation it was written for, and what style it uses, we open up its content — and find that its message connects directly with our own. 1 Timothy and 2 Timothy are not museum pieces. They are urgent, gospel-driven letters for churches in every age that want to guard the truth and grow in Christ.

chapters.chapterTitle: How to Encounter a Bible Passage (Tools)

elements.text: Units 2 and 3 taught principles for understanding the Bible and applied them to 1 and 2 Timothy. A central principle was this: we must discover what God has said in the past before we can declare it today. This unit focuses on that discovery. We will learn the first steps of a process called ’Scripture Sculpture’ — a series of steps that take us from reading a Bible passage to preaching it. The first four steps are taught here. The final steps, which move from understanding to teaching, are covered in Unit 13.

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Explain how Unit 3 relates to Unit 4
2. List the first four steps of the Scripture Sculpture process
3. Begin to use the four steps for encountering a Bible passage
4. Respond to God’s word in 2 Timothy 1:6-7
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.text: 4.1 Introducing ’Scripture Sculpture’

elements.question: What do you understand by the word ’sculpture’? What does a sculptor do, and what does their craft involve?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what sculptors make and how they work — carving wood, shaping clay, working with stone…
elements.sampleAnswer: Sculpture is an art and a craft. A sculptor takes a raw material — wood, clay, stone or metal — and works it carefully to bring out a beautiful or meaningful form. The sculptor does not invent the material; they work with what is already there, using tools and skill to reveal what is within it. The process takes patience, attention to detail, and a clear vision of what the finished work should look like.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between a sculptor and a painter. A painter adds to a blank surface; a sculptor works with — and within — what is already there.

elements.text: Ramesh Richard, who developed the Scripture Sculpture method, explains the name like this: ’The Bible is what God has made. Sermons are what we make with what God has made.’ Like a sculptor working with wood or stone, the craft of teaching the Bible means taking what God has given and using it to shape every aspect of our preparation and preaching.

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>Make every effort to present yourself before God as a proven worker who does not need to be ashamed, teaching the message of truth accurately.

elements.question: What picture does Paul use in 2 Timothy 2:15, and what does it suggest about how we should handle the Bible?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the image of a ’proven worker’ — what kind of work is Paul describing, and what does it require?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul uses the image of a skilled worker — someone who has been tested and approved, who handles their material with precision and care. The phrase ’teaching the message of truth accurately’ carries the idea of cutting or dividing something cleanly and correctly. Just as a good craftsman works with the grain and structure of the wood rather than against it, a good Bible teacher works with the shape and structure of the Scriptures. The goal is not to impose our ideas onto the text but to let the text’s own form and argument speak clearly.
elements.questionHint: The word translated ’accurately’ in the NET carries the idea of cutting straight — like a carpenter making a clean joint. What does that imply about how careless or sloppy Bible handling looks to God?

elements.text: It is also clear that preaching and prayer belong together. In all our preparation we must obey Paul’s command to ’pray continually’ (1 Thess. 5:17). Prayer is not one step among others — it is the atmosphere in which every step takes place.
4.2 Four Steps for Encountering a Passage of the Bible

The Scripture Sculpture process uses a powerful image: a living carving of a person, with flesh and bones, a heart and a brain. This is fitting because God’s word is a living word that comes to us and speaks to us. The four steps build on each other from the ground up. Begin at the bottom — with prayer and reading — and work upwards.

elements.alt: Diagram of the four Scripture Sculpture steps stacked vertically: Start (Pray and Read the Passage) at the bottom, then Step 1 Flesh (Study the Details), Step 2 Skeleton (Outline the Structure), Step 3 Heart (Identify the Message), and Step 4 Brain (Understand the Purpose) at the top. Arrows point upward between each step.
elements.caption: The four steps of Scripture Sculpture — building from prayer and reading upward to purpose

elements.eyebrow: Four Steps for Encountering a Passage — in Detail
elements.term: Scripture Sculpture: the first four steps
elements.question: What does each step involve, and what will you be able to do at the end of it?
elements.answer:
Step 1: FLESH — Study the DETAILS of the text
At the end of this step you will be familiar with the key components of the passage. You need to: (a) Set the text in context; (b) See the details; (c) Seek the meaning; (d) Summarise the findings.

Step 2: SKELETON — Outline the STRUCTURE of the text
At the end of this step you will be able to outline the structure of the passage and see how the author makes his point. You need to: (a) Note the various ’bones’ in the passage; (b) Create an outline, having identified the ’big bones’.

Step 3: HEART — Identify the MESSAGE of the text
At the end of this step you will be able to write out the author’s main point in one or two sentences. You need to: (a) State the theme of the passage; (b) Capture the message of the passage.

Step 4: BRAIN — Understand the PURPOSE of the text
At the end of this step you will be able to explain the purpose of the passage and will begin to make connections for teaching it today. You need to: (a) State the purpose of the passage; (b) Make connections for teaching it today.

elements.question: In your own words, how do the four steps build on each other? Why do you think the process starts with ’flesh’ (details) rather than jumping straight to the ’heart’ (message)?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what happens if you try to find the message of a passage without first studying its details carefully…
elements.sampleAnswer: The steps build on each other because understanding a passage is a progressive process. You cannot reliably identify the message (heart) of a passage if you have not first observed its details carefully (flesh) and traced its structure (skeleton). Starting with the details prevents us from jumping to conclusions or reading our own ideas into the text. The flesh grounds us in what is actually there; the skeleton shows us how the pieces relate; the heart emerges naturally from that careful observation rather than being imposed from outside. Moving from details to structure to message to purpose mirrors the way genuine understanding develops.
elements.questionHint: Think about what might go wrong if someone tried to preach a passage after only skimming it once. What would they likely miss, and what might they get wrong?

elements.text: 4.3 The Four Steps Applied to 2 Timothy 1:6-7

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>Because of this I remind you to rekindle God’s gift that you possess through the laying on of my hands. <sup>7</sup>For the Spirit God gave us does not make us timid but gives us power, love, and self-control.

elements.text: Before going any further, read the passage carefully — more than once. Read it slowly. Read it aloud if you can. Scripture Sculpture always begins with patient, prayerful reading.
Step 1: FLESH — Study the Details
1a) Set the Text in Context

Keep the general information about 2 Timothy from Unit 3 in mind. A passage can only be properly understood in the context of the book as a whole — trying to understand a passage without knowing its book is like trying to get to know a family member without knowing their wider family. Use the same three words from Unit 3: story, situation, and style.

elements.passageText:
3I am thankful to God, whom I have served with a clear conscience as my ancestors did, when I remember you in my prayers as I do constantly night and day. 4As I remember your tears, I long to see you, so that I may be filled with joy. 5I recall your sincere faith that was alive first in your grandmother Lois and your mother Eunice, and I am confident that it is in you too.
6Because of this I remind you to rekindle God’s gift that you possess through the laying on of my hands. 7For the Spirit God gave us does not make us timid but gives us power, love, and self-control. 8So do not be ashamed of the testimony about our Lord or of me, a prisoner for his sake, but by God’s power accept your share of suffering for the gospel.

elements.question: STORY: Where does 2 Tim 1:6-7 come in the argument of the book? What comes immediately before and after it, and how does that help you understand these two verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at what comes before (vv. 3-5) and after (v. 8). What is Paul building up to, and what does v. 6 follow on from?
elements.sampleAnswer: Verses 3-5 establish Paul’s confidence in the reality of Timothy’s faith — a sincere faith that was alive first in his grandmother Lois and his mother Eunice. Verse 6 then opens with ’Because of this’ — meaning Paul’s appeal to rekindle God’s gift flows directly from his confidence in Timothy’s genuine faith. The appeal is not to someone who might not be a Christian; it is to someone whose faith Paul is sure of. Verse 8 then draws the consequence: ‘So do not be ashamed...’ — verses 6-7 are therefore the grounds for the command in verse 8. Understanding this flow shows that the call to rekindle gifts is not about earning God’s favour but about acting on what God has already given.
elements.questionHint: The phrase ’Because of this’ in v. 6 is a key link word. What does it connect back to, and what does it connect forward to?

elements.question: SITUATION: What do you notice about 2 Tim 1:6-7 in relation to what you already know about the situation in 2 Timothy — the people, problems, and places involved?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what you learnt in Unit 3 about Paul’s situation and Timothy’s situation. How does that context illuminate these two verses?
elements.sampleAnswer: From Unit 3 we know that Paul is writing from prison, facing death, and that many have deserted him. Timothy is a younger leader who must carry on the gospel work without Paul present. In that context, the call to ’rekindle God’s gift’ and the reminder that the Spirit gives power rather than timidity takes on great urgency. Timothy is not being called to step up in easy circumstances — he is being called to courage in a situation where fear would be a very natural response. The contrast between ’timid’ and ’power, love, and self-control’ speaks directly to the real pressures Timothy is facing.
elements.questionHint: Why might Timothy have been particularly tempted towards timidity at this moment in his ministry? What was happening around him that could have caused fear or hesitation?

elements.question: STYLE: What do you notice about 2 Tim 1:6-8 that shows it is part of a letter? What features of letter-writing do you see?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the personal tone, the direct address, the way Paul builds an argument — what marks this out as a letter rather than a sermon or a law code?
elements.sampleAnswer: Several features mark this as a personal letter. Paul uses direct personal address (‘I remind you’, ‘you possess’, ‘do not be ashamed’) — he is speaking to one named person in a specific situation. The tone is warm and fatherly, not formal or impersonal. The passage builds a personal argument: ‘I know your faith is real (vv. 3-5), therefore I remind you to act on it (v. 6), here is why you can (v. 7), and here is what I am asking you to do (v. 8).’ Letters are ’occasional’ — written for a particular occasion — and the urgency of this letter reflects the very particular and pressing occasion: Paul’s imprisonment and approaching death.
elements.questionHint: Compare this passage with, say, a passage from Leviticus or Proverbs. What is different about the way Paul speaks? What makes a letter feel different from a law or a proverb?

elements.text: 1b) See the Details of the Text

Now look closely at the words of 2 Tim 1:6-7 themselves. You are looking for three kinds of detail:

1. Repeated and related words and phrases — words or ideas that appear more than once, or that cluster together around a theme.
2. Other important, complicated, or unusual words and phrases — anything that stands out, surprises you, or needs explaining.
3. Connections and links between words and phrases — logical connectors like ’for’, ‘therefore’, ‘but’, ‘because’, ‘so’; and contrasts like ’not… but…’.

In a paper-based setting you would mark these with different colours — green for repeated words, red for important or unusual words, blue for links and connections. In this study, use the questions below to capture the same observations in writing. Be selective: if you mark everything, you have marked nothing.

elements.question: LIST the important words, phrases, and relationships you notice in 2 Tim 1:6-7. What stands out as you read carefully? Include repeated words, unusual phrases, and any linking or contrasting words.
elements.answerPlaceholder: List the words and phrases — one per line if that helps. Don’t explain them yet, just notice and list them…
elements.sampleAnswer:
‘Because of this’ (linking word — looks back)
’I remind you’ (personal, direct command)
’rekindle’ / ’fan into flame’ (the main thing Paul calls Timothy to do)
’God’s gift’ / ’God gave’ / ’gives’ (repeated idea of gift from God)
’laying on of my hands’ (unusual phrase — needs explaining)
’For’ (v. 7 — introduces a reason or explanation)
’Spirit’ (who is the subject of v. 7)
’timid’ and ’power’ (contrasting words)
’does not… but…’ (contrast structure)
’power, love, and self-control’ (a trio — each word deserves attention)
elements.questionHint: Read slowly and read more than once. Try reading it aloud. What words would you naturally want to underline or circle? Don’t evaluate yet — just list what you see.

elements.text: 1c) Seek the Meaning from the Details

elements.question: EXPLAIN the words, phrases, and relationships you listed. What do they mean in their context — both in today’s language and in the original setting of the passage? What is their significance in the argument Paul is building?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through your list from the previous question. For each word or phrase: what does it mean? Why does it matter here?
elements.sampleAnswer:
‘Because of this’ — links back to v. 5: Paul’s confidence in Timothy’s sincere faith is the basis for the appeal that follows.
’I remind you’ — Paul is not teaching Timothy something new; he is calling him back to something he already knows but needs to act on again.
’Rekindle’ / ’fan into flame’ — the image is of a fire that has died down and needs to be stirred back to life. The gift has not gone, but Timothy needs to actively renew it.
’God’s gift’ / ’God gave’ / ’gives’ — the repetition emphasises that the initiative is God’s; Timothy is responding to what God has already given, not generating something himself.
’Laying on of my hands’ — a public act of commissioning and recognition; 1 Tim 4:14 shows it involved the whole eldership. This gives Timothy confidence: others have publicly affirmed his calling.
’For’ — introduces the reason for the command in v. 6: because of the character of the Spirit, Timothy can and must rekindle his gifts.
’Timid’ vs ’power, love, and self-control’ — the contrast is total. God’s Spirit does not produce cowardice; he produces exactly what Timothy needs to fulfil his calling. Each quality in the trio balances the others: power without love becomes harsh; love without self-control becomes sentimental; self-control without power becomes cold.
elements.questionHint: For each item on your list, ask: What does this word or phrase actually mean? What would be lost if it were removed? How does it connect to the words around it?

elements.text: 1d) Summarise Your Findings

elements.question: Drawing on your work so far, summarise your main findings from Step 1. What are the most important ideas you want to carry forward into the next steps?
elements.answerPlaceholder: What are the two or three most important things you have discovered about this passage so far?
elements.sampleAnswer:
Three main ideas emerge from the initial study:
1. Paul is confident that Timothy’s faith in Christ is genuine (from the context in vv. 3-5). This confidence is the foundation for everything that follows.
2. Because of that confidence, Paul reminds Timothy to rekindle his gifts — to actively renew his passion and engagement with the ministry God has given him. The gifts are real; Timothy must not let them lie dormant.
3. The greatest resource for this renewal is God’s Holy Spirit, who has been given to Timothy (and to all believers). The Spirit does not produce timidity — he produces power, love, and self-control. These three qualities are precisely what Timothy needs for his situation.
elements.questionHint: Imagine you had to explain to someone else the three most important things this passage is saying. What would you choose? Your summary should be something you could carry into the next steps as a foundation.

elements.text: Step 2: SKELETON — Outline the Structure

You are now aiming to discover how Paul has structured his writing — his flow of thought — so that you can understand the message it carries. The clues are in the details you have already noted. Look especially at two things: the links (logical connectors that show how ideas relate) and the themes (the way the main ideas are arranged).

elements.question: LINKS: What important linking words contribute to the structure of 2 Tim 1:6-7? How do they hold the passage together and show the logic of Paul’s argument?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at ’because of this’, ‘for’, ‘not… but…’ — what does each link connect, and what does that tell you about the structure?
elements.sampleAnswer:
‘Because of this’ (v. 6) — links the appeal back to v. 5. The command to rekindle gifts is grounded in Paul’s confidence in Timothy’s sincere faith. The logic is: ‘Your faith is real, therefore act on it.’
’For’ (v. 7) — introduces a further reason for the command in v. 6. Timothy should rekindle his gifts not only because his faith is genuine, but also because of the character of the Spirit who has been given to him.
’Not… but…’ (v. 7) — creates a direct contrast between what the Spirit does not do (produce timidity) and what he does do (give power, love, and self-control). This contrast is the heart of the reason Paul gives.
elements.questionHint: Linking words are the joints of the skeleton — they hold the bones together and show how one part connects to another. What would the passage look like if you removed all the linking words? What would be lost?

elements.question: THEMES: How are the main ideas arranged in 2 Tim 1:6-7? Can you identify the major movements or sections in these two verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to divide the passage into its main parts. What is Paul doing in v. 6a? In v. 6b? In v. 7?
elements.sampleAnswer:
The passage moves through three main ideas:
1. An introduction (v. 6a: ‘Because of this I remind you’) — Paul establishes the basis for his appeal: he is reminding Timothy of something rooted in the confidence already expressed in v. 5.
2. A command (v. 6b: ‘to rekindle God’s gift… through the laying on of my hands’) — Timothy is called to action: rekindle the gift. The reference to the laying on of hands adds a note of confidence — this was a public, witnessed act of commissioning.
3. A reason (v. 7: ‘For the Spirit God gave us…’) — Paul explains why Timothy can and must obey: the Spirit he has received is not a spirit of timidity but of power, love, and self-control.
elements.questionHint: Try to label each section with a single phrase that captures its function in the argument: is it introducing, commanding, explaining, contrasting, or concluding?

elements.question: OUTLINE: Having identified the ’big bones’, write a simple structural outline of 2 Tim 1:6-7. What are the main sections and how would you label them?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Write your outline in 2-3 numbered points. Each point should capture one main section of the passage and explain what Paul is doing there…
elements.sampleAnswer:
1. The introduction to confidence in God and his gifts (v. 6a): Timothy can be confident in his faith because Paul has already assured him it is sincere (v. 5).
2. The command to renew confidence in God and his gifts (v. 6b): Timothy can be confident that he has the God-given gifts for ministry — therefore he must rekindle them.
3. The reason to renew confidence in God and his gifts (v. 7): God’s Spirit is powerful, loving, and self-controlled — and we have the presence of this confidence-building Spirit in our lives.
elements.questionHint: A good outline is simple enough to remember. Aim for 2-3 points. Each point should describe what Paul is doing, not just what he is saying.

elements.text: Step 3: HEART — Identify the Message

A simple way to identify the message of a passage is to note two things: the author’s theme (what he is talking about) and the message (what he is saying about that theme). The Bible is ’God preaching’ to his people — so a biblical message is more than information. It tells us something important and practical.

elements.question: THEME: What is the main thing Paul is talking about in 2 Tim 1:6-7? State the theme of the passage as simply and clearly as you can — ideally in a short phrase.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to capture the theme in 3-6 words — the one thing this passage keeps coming back to…
elements.sampleAnswer: The theme is: renewing confidence in God and his gifts for ministry.
elements.questionHint: The theme is not the message — it is the subject the message is about. If the message were a sentence, the theme would be its subject. What is the subject of this passage?

elements.question: MESSAGE: What is Paul saying about that theme? Complete this sentence in a way that captures the main point of the passage: ‘Paul tells Timothy that…’
elements.answerPlaceholder: Complete the sentence: ‘Paul tells Timothy that…’ — aim for one or two clear sentences, without technical words, that even a child could understand.
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul tells Timothy to renew his confidence in God and his gifts for ministry — because God’s Spirit, who has been given to him, produces not timidity but power, love, and self-control.
elements.questionHint: The message should flow naturally from the theme. It completes the thought: the theme says ’what about?’; the message says ’what about it?’. Try to say it in a way a non-theologian would understand.

elements.text: Step 4: BRAIN — Understand the Purpose

The purpose statement answers a different question from the message statement. The message says what is being said; the purpose says why it is being said. Both are essential when it comes to making connections for teaching today.

elements.question: PURPOSE: Why is Paul making this point to Timothy? What is he trying to achieve by saying this, in this situation, at this moment?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about Timothy’s situation — what does he need, and why does Paul think this particular reminder will help him?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul is telling Timothy to renew his confidence in God and his gifts because Timothy is facing an urgent and demanding task — one that he cannot overcome with his own abilities and strength. Paul is in prison; many have deserted him; Timothy must carry on the gospel work largely alone. In that situation, fear and timidity are real dangers. Paul’s purpose is to remind Timothy that the resources he needs are not human resources — they come from God’s Spirit, who has already been given to him. The purpose is to prevent Timothy from shrinking back and to strengthen him for courageous gospel ministry.
elements.questionHint: The purpose explains why this message was needed — what problem or danger it addresses. What would have happened if Paul had not written these two verses?

elements.question: CONNECTIONS: What connections do you see between the purpose of 2 Tim 1:6-7 and your own situation and congregation today? Capture your answer as a statement or question that brings the passage into the present.
elements.answerPlaceholder: What do you and your people need to hear from this passage? Why do they need to hear it now?
elements.sampleAnswer: One connecting question might be: ‘In the face of all the daily demands and difficulties we face, how can we renew our confidence in God and his gifts for ministry?’ Like Timothy, many church leaders and Christians today face situations that could produce fear — opposition, isolation, a sense of inadequacy, the weight of difficult circumstances. The message of 2 Tim 1:6-7 is not just for Timothy in a first-century prison; it is for every believer who is tempted to shrink back rather than step forward in God’s strength. The Spirit who was given to Timothy has been given to us too.
elements.questionHint: Think about the people in your church or community. Who is most at risk of the kind of timidity Paul is addressing? What would it mean for them to ’rekindle’ their gifts?

elements.text: 4.4 Pulling It All Together

The two callouts below pull together all four steps of the Scripture Sculpture process as applied to 2 Tim 1:6-7. These are worked examples — model answers that show what careful, prayerful engagement with the passage can produce. Compare them with your own answers above.

elements.term: Step 1: FLESH — Study the Details of 2 Tim 1:6-7
elements.question: Tap to see the full worked example for Step 1.
elements.answer:
a) Set the passage in context

Story: Verses 3-5 emphasise the reality of Timothy’s faith. Verse 6 begins with ’Because of this…’ — Paul’s confidence in Timothy’s faith is the basis for his appeal to ’rekindle God’s gift.’ Verse 8 then draws the consequence: ‘So do not be ashamed…’ Verses 6-7 must therefore give Timothy reason for courage and confidence.

Situation: The passage communicates the close, personal nature of Paul and Timothy’s relationship, and shows that Timothy needs encouragement in a demanding situation.

Style: A section of a letter, which builds a clear personal argument — it explains and applies the gospel to a specific person in a specific crisis.

b) See the details

’For this reason…’ / ’remind’ (links with ’remember’, ‘recalling’, ‘reminded’ in previous verses) / ’rekindle’ (the main thing Paul calls Timothy to do) / ’God’s gift’, ‘God gave’, ‘gives’ (repeated) / ’laying on of hands’ (complex, needs explaining) / ’For’ (introduces explanation) / ’Spirit’ / ’timid’ and ’power’ (contrasting words) / ’does not… but…’ (contrast) / ’power, love, and self-control’ (important trio)

c) Seek the meaning

’For this reason’ — links back: ‘based on what I have said about your faith…’ / ’remind’ — Paul has been remembering things; Timothy needs to remember something important too / ’rekindle’ — Timothy must renew his passion and reignite the gifts he has / ’God’s gift / gave / gives’ — Paul encourages Timothy that God has given him all he needs / ’laying on of hands’ — 1 Tim 4:14 ties the gifts to a public act of commissioning with witnesses / ’Spirit’ — God’s empowering presence / ’timid’ vs ’power’ — personal weakness contrasted with the Spirit’s strength / ’not… but…’ — underlines the contrast / ’power, love, and self-control’ — the Spirit has these qualities and brings them to Timothy; each needs the other two.

d) Summary of findings

Paul is convinced that Timothy’s faith in Christ is genuine. Because of this confidence Paul reminds Timothy to do something important: he must renew his confidence in God and his gifts. The greatest gift of all is God’s Holy Spirit — he is powerful, loving, and self-controlled.

elements.term: Steps 2–4: SKELETON, HEART, BRAIN — 2 Tim 1:6-7
elements.question: Tap to see the full worked examples for Steps 2, 3, and 4.
elements.answer:
Step 2: SKELETON — Outline the Structure

Links: ’Because of this’ links the passage to v. 5 — the confidence Timothy can have in his own God-given faith. ’For’ (v. 7) links back to v. 6 — Timothy is to rekindle his gifts not only because of the confidence he can have in his own faith, but also because of the character of God’s Holy Spirit who has been given to him. ’Not… but…’ creates the contrast at the heart of v. 7.

Themes: Verse 6 opens with an introduction (‘Because of this’), rooted in Paul’s confidence in Timothy’s sincere faith. It continues with a command (‘rekindle God’s gift’) and adds a note of confidence (‘through the laying on of my hands’ — a public, witnessed event). Verse 7 gives a further reason for Timothy to use his gifts with confidence rather than fear.

Outline (the ’big bones’):
1. Introduction to confidence in God and his gifts: Timothy can be confident in his faith (v. 6a).
2. Command to renew confidence in God and his gifts: Timothy must rekindle his God-given gifts for ministry (v. 6b).
3. Reason to renew confidence in God and his gifts: God’s Spirit produces power, love, and self-control — not timidity (v. 7).

Step 3: HEART — Identify the Message

Theme: Renewing confidence in God and his gifts for ministry.

Message: Paul tells Timothy to renew his confidence in God and his gifts for ministry — because the Spirit God has given produces power, love, and self-control, not timidity.

Step 4: BRAIN — Understand the Purpose

Purpose: Paul tells Timothy to renew his confidence in God and his gifts because he is facing an urgent and demanding task — one he cannot overcome with his own abilities and strength.

Connection for today: In the face of all the daily demands and difficulties we face, how can we renew our confidence in God and his gifts for ministry?

elements.text: 4.5 Responding to 2 Timothy 1:6-7

We will learn more about how to preach and teach from Bible passages in Unit 13 — which will use 2 Tim 1:6-7 as its worked example. But it is important that we respond to the passages we encounter before we think about teaching them. The three questions below invite you to do that.

elements.question: How did the message of 2 Tim 1:6-7 speak to you personally? What did you learn, or what did you need to be reminded of?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: How did the purpose of the passage help you to apply God’s word to your own life? What change might this passage make to your thinking or living?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: In your own words, state the message you would share with others from this passage. What is the main thing you want people to understand?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: In this unit we have learnt the first four steps of Scripture Sculpture and applied them to 2 Timothy 1:6-7. These steps — flesh, skeleton, heart, and brain — give us a disciplined, prayerful way of encountering any passage of Scripture: starting with careful observation of the details, moving through structure and message, and arriving at a clear understanding of the passage’s purpose. These same steps will be used throughout the BUILD curriculum as we constantly move from understanding texts to teaching them.

In this unit you will apply the four steps from Unit 4 to a new passage from 2 Timothy — 1:8-12. You are now the one doing the work. Keep referring back to the four steps as you go, and take your time with each one. You might want to work through this on your own, with a friend, or in a small group.

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Follow and apply the four steps of Encountering a Bible Passage to a passage from 2 Timothy yourself
2. Share the message of 2 Timothy 1:8-12 with a group of people today
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.text: The Passage: 2 Timothy 1:8-12

Before you begin the four steps, read the passage carefully — more than once. Read it slowly. If you are working in a group, take it in turns to read it aloud between each step and listen for different things each time. Scripture Sculpture always begins with patient, prayerful reading.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>So do not be ashamed of the testimony about our Lord or of me, a prisoner for his sake, but by God’s power accept your share of suffering for the gospel. <sup>9</sup>He has saved us and called us with a holy calling, not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace, granted to us in Christ Jesus before time began. <sup>10</sup>But now this has been revealed through the appearing of our Savior Christ Jesus, who abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel! <sup>11</sup>For this gospel I was appointed a preacher and apostle and teacher. <sup>12</sup>Because of this, in fact, I suffer as I do. But I am not ashamed, because I know the one in whom my faith is set and I am convinced that he is able to protect what has been entrusted to me until that day.

elements.text: Step 1: FLESH — Study the Details
1a) Set the Text in Context

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>Because of this I remind you to rekindle God’s gift that you possess through the laying on of my hands. <sup>7</sup>For the Spirit God gave us does not make us timid but gives us power, love, and self-control. <sup>8</sup>So do not be ashamed of the testimony about our Lord or of me, a prisoner for his sake, but by God’s power accept your share of suffering for the gospel. <sup>9</sup>He has saved us and called us with a holy calling, not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace, granted to us in Christ Jesus before time began. <sup>10</sup>But now this has been revealed through the appearing of our Savior Christ Jesus, who abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel! <sup>11</sup>For this gospel I was appointed a preacher and apostle and teacher. <sup>12</sup>Because of this, in fact, I suffer as I do. But I am not ashamed, because I know the one in whom my faith is set and I am convinced that he is able to protect what has been entrusted to me until that day. <sup>13</sup>Hold to the standard of sound words that you heard from me and do so with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus. <sup>14</sup>Protect that good thing entrusted to you, through the Holy Spirit who lives within us.

elements.question: STORY: Where does 2 Tim 1:8-12 come in the argument of the book? What comes immediately before and after it, and how does knowing that help you understand these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at what comes before (vv. 6-7) and after (vv. 13-14). What has Paul just said, and where is he heading?
elements.sampleAnswer: Verses 6-7 (which we studied in Unit 4) contain Paul’s appeal to Timothy to rekindle God’s gift, grounded in the fact that the Spirit produces power, love, and self-control — not timidity. Verse 8 then opens with ’So’ — drawing a direct conclusion from what has just been said: because the Spirit gives power rather than timidity, Timothy must not be ashamed. The passage therefore flows naturally from the previous unit’s encouragement into a specific, demanding application: share in suffering for the gospel. After our passage, vv. 13-14 draw the consequences: hold to sound teaching and guard the good deposit. The passage sits at the heart of a sustained appeal from Paul to Timothy to stand firm and not shrink back.
elements.questionHint: The word ’So’ at the start of v. 8 is a key connector. What does it draw a conclusion from? And what does the passage flow into in vv. 13-14?

elements.question: SITUATION: What do you notice about 2 Tim 1:8-12 in relation to what you already know about the situation in 2 Timothy — the people, problems, and places involved?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about Paul’s imprisonment, Timothy’s task, and the pressures on the early church. How does the situation shape what Paul says here?
elements.sampleAnswer: The situation is reflected directly in the language. Paul mentions that he is a prisoner (v. 8) and explicitly says he is suffering (v. 12). Yet he insists he is ’not ashamed’ — which tells us that shame and fear of suffering were real temptations in this situation. For Timothy, being associated with an imprisoned, potentially disgraced apostle carried social and personal risk. Paul’s reference to his own example (‘I suffer as I do... but I am not ashamed’) is deliberate: he is modelling what he is asking Timothy to do. The situation makes the appeal both urgent and personal.
elements.questionHint: Why might Timothy have been tempted to be ’ashamed’ of Paul? What did it cost, socially and personally, to be associated with a prisoner in the Roman world?

elements.question: STYLE: What features of this passage show that it is part of a personal letter? What do you notice about Paul’s tone and the way he builds his argument?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the personal address, the use of ’I’ and ’we’, the emotional register, and the way Paul moves from command to gospel to personal testimony…
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage is unmistakably a personal letter. Paul moves fluidly between direct address (‘do not be ashamed’, ‘accept your share of suffering’), shared confession (‘He has saved us and called us’), and personal testimony (‘I was appointed... I suffer as I do... I am not ashamed... I know the one in whom my faith is set’). The tone is warm, urgent, and deeply personal — this is not abstract theology but a father writing to a son in a crisis. Paul also demonstrates the very courage he is calling for: he testifies openly to his own suffering and his own confidence in God, making himself an example of the gospel he is commending.
elements.questionHint: Count how many times Paul uses ’I’, ‘we’, and ’us’ in vv. 8-12. What does the blend of personal testimony and shared gospel confession tell you about how Paul uses his own experience as part of his argument?

elements.text: 1b) See the Details of the Text

Read 2 Tim 1:8-12 again carefully. Look for: repeated and related words and phrases; important, complicated, or unusual words and phrases; and connections and links between words and phrases (words like ’so’, ‘but’, ‘for’, ‘because’, ‘not… but…’). Be selective — list what genuinely stands out.

elements.question: LIST the important words, phrases, and relationships you notice in 2 Tim 1:8-12. What stands out as you read carefully?
elements.answerPlaceholder: List words and phrases — one per line if that helps. Don’t explain them yet, just notice and list them…
elements.sampleAnswer:
‘So’ (v. 8 — connecting word, draws conclusion from vv. 6-7)
’do not be ashamed’ (v. 8 — key command; echoed in v. 12 ’I am not ashamed’)
’testimony about our Lord’ / ’gospel’ (vv. 8, 10, 11 — repeated theme)
’prisoner’ / ’suffering’ (vv. 8, 12 — repeated, personal)
’by God’s power’ (v. 8 — the means of endurance)
’He has saved us and called us’ (v. 9 — gospel summary)
’not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace’ (v. 9 — key contrast)
’before time began’ (v. 9 — striking phrase about the eternal origin of grace)
’But now’ (v. 10 — turning point: from eternity to history)
’appearing of our Saviour, Christ Jesus’ (v. 10 — the incarnation)
’abolished death’ / ’brought life and immortality to light’ (v. 10 — dramatic contrast)
’gospel’ (vv. 8, 10, 11 — three times)
’preacher and apostle and teacher’ (v. 11 — Paul’s triple role)
’For this gospel I was appointed’ (v. 11 — links Paul’s suffering to the gospel)
’I know the one in whom my faith is set’ (v. 12 — confidence)
’convinced that he is able to protect what has been entrusted to me’ (v. 12 — trust)
’that day’ (v. 12 — eschatological horizon)
elements.questionHint: Try reading the passage three times, each time looking for a different category: first for repeated words, then for unusual or important phrases, then for linking and contrasting words. What do you see each time?

elements.text: 1c) Seek the Meaning from the Details

elements.question: EXPLAIN the words, phrases, and relationships you listed. What do they mean in their context — both in today’s language and in the original setting? What is their significance in the argument Paul is building?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through your list from the previous question. For each word or phrase: what does it mean, and why does it matter here?
elements.sampleAnswer:
‘So’ — draws the consequence from Unit 4’s passage: because the Spirit gives power not timidity, the conclusion is ’do not be ashamed’.
’Do not be ashamed’ / ’I am not ashamed’ — shame was a powerful social force in the Roman world. Being associated with a prisoner could bring disgrace. Paul commands Timothy not to yield to that pressure, and models it himself.
’By God’s power’ — the means of endurance is not Timothy’s own strength but God’s. This echoes v. 7: the Spirit gives power.
’He has saved us and called us’ (v. 9) — a compressed gospel summary. Salvation is presented as both a past act (‘has saved’) and an ongoing calling.
’Not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace’ — the contrast is total: salvation rests entirely on God’s initiative, not human merit. This is foundational to why suffering for the gospel makes sense — it is God’s gospel, not ours.
’Before time began’ — grace was planned in eternity, before creation. This gives the gospel cosmic weight and permanence.
’But now’ (v. 10) — a hinge phrase. What was planned in eternity has now broken into history through the incarnation.
’Abolished death’ / ’brought life and immortality to light’ — vivid, dramatic language. Death has been defeated; what was hidden is now revealed. The gospel does not merely improve life — it conquers death.
’Gospel’ (three times) — the repetition is deliberate. The gospel is the reason for Paul’s appointment, his suffering, and his confidence.
’Preacher and apostle and teacher’ — three roles, each emphasising a different dimension of gospel ministry: proclamation, authoritative witness, and instruction.
’I know the one in whom my faith is set’ — personal, relational confidence in God himself, not merely in a set of beliefs.
’That day’ — the day of Christ’s return and final judgement. Paul’s horizon is eschatological: he endures in the present because he is confident about the future.
elements.questionHint: For the most important phrases, ask: What would be lost if this phrase were not here? What does it add to the argument? Try to explain each phrase in plain language that someone with no theological training could understand.

elements.text: 1d) Summarise Your Findings

elements.question: Drawing on your work so far, summarise your main findings from Step 1. What are the most important ideas you want to carry forward into the next steps?
elements.answerPlaceholder: What are the two or three most important things you have discovered about this passage so far?
elements.sampleAnswer:
Three main ideas emerge:
1. The command not to be ashamed — Timothy is called to accept his share of suffering for the gospel, by God’s power, not his own. Paul models this himself: he is suffering but not ashamed.
2. The gospel itself is the foundation and the reason — vv. 9-10 provide a compressed but rich summary of the gospel: a grace planned before time, revealed in Christ, who abolished death and brought life to light. This gospel is what makes suffering worthwhile.
3. Paul’s personal confidence is the pastoral heart of the passage — ‘I know the one in whom my faith is set.’ Paul’s endurance is not grounded in circumstances or in his own strength, but in his knowledge of and trust in God himself, who will guard what has been entrusted until the last day.
elements.questionHint: Imagine you had to explain to someone else the two or three most important things this passage is about. What would you choose? Your summary should be something you can carry into Steps 2-4 as a foundation.

elements.text: Step 2: SKELETON — Outline the Structure

elements.question: LINKS: What important linking words contribute to the structure of 2 Tim 1:8-12? How do they show the logic of Paul’s argument?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at ’so’, ‘but’, ‘for’, ‘because’, ‘not… but…’ — what does each link connect, and what does that tell you about the structure?
elements.sampleAnswer:
‘So’ (v. 8) — links back to vv. 6-7. The command not to be ashamed is the conclusion drawn from the Spirit’s character (power, not timidity).
’Not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace’ (v. 9) — a contrast that is central to the argument: salvation is entirely God’s initiative. This makes the gospel worth suffering for.
’But now’ (v. 10) — a pivotal hinge between eternity (v. 9, ‘before time began’) and history (v. 10, ‘through the appearing of Christ Jesus’). It marks the turning point of the gospel story.
’For this gospel’ (v. 11) — links Paul’s appointment and suffering directly to the gospel just described. His suffering makes sense only because of the gospel.
’Because of this’ (v. 12) — links Paul’s suffering back to his appointment in v. 11. The suffering is a consequence of faithful gospel ministry.
’But I am not ashamed, because’ (v. 12) — introduces the reason for Paul’s confidence in the face of suffering: his knowledge of and trust in God.
elements.questionHint: The linking words form the joints of the skeleton — they hold the argument together. Try tracing the logic: what follows from what? Each connector should reveal a step in Paul’s reasoning.

elements.question: THEMES: How are the main ideas arranged in 2 Tim 1:8-12? Can you identify the major movements or sections in these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to divide the passage into its main parts. What is Paul doing in v. 8? In vv. 9-10? In v. 11? In v. 12?
elements.sampleAnswer:
The passage moves through four main sections:
1. The command (v. 8): Do not be ashamed — join in suffering for the gospel, by God’s power.
2. The gospel foundation (vv. 9-10): The reason suffering for the gospel is worthwhile — God’s grace, planned in eternity, revealed in Christ who abolished death and brought life to light.
3. Paul’s appointment (v. 11): Paul’s role as preacher, apostle, and teacher directly connects him to this gospel — and therefore to suffering for it.
4. Paul’s confidence (v. 12): Despite suffering, Paul is not ashamed — because he knows and trusts God, who is able to guard what has been entrusted until the last day.
elements.questionHint: Try labelling each section with a single phrase that captures its function: is it commanding, explaining, grounding, testifying? What is Paul doing in each part?

elements.question: OUTLINE: Having identified the ’big bones’, write a simple structural outline of 2 Tim 1:8-12. What are the main sections and how would you label them?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Write your outline in 3-4 numbered points. Each point should capture one main section of the passage and explain what Paul is doing there…
elements.sampleAnswer:
1. The command to courage (v. 8): Timothy must not be ashamed of the gospel or of Paul — he must accept his share of suffering, by God’s power.
2. The gospel that makes courage possible (vv. 9-10): God’s grace, purposed before time and revealed in Christ, who abolished death and brought life to light through the gospel.
3. The appointment that makes suffering inevitable (v. 11): Paul was appointed as preacher, apostle, and teacher of this gospel — and that appointment is why he suffers.
4. The confidence that makes shame impossible (v. 12): Paul knows the one he trusts, and is convinced that God will guard what has been entrusted to him until the last day.
elements.questionHint: A good outline is simple enough to remember and preach from. Aim for 3-4 points. Each point should both describe what Paul is doing and hint at what it means for the listener.

elements.text: Step 3: HEART — Identify the Message

elements.question: THEME: What is the main thing Paul is talking about in 2 Tim 1:8-12? State the theme of the passage as simply and clearly as you can — ideally in a short phrase.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to capture the theme in 3-6 words — the one subject this passage keeps coming back to…
elements.sampleAnswer: The theme is: not being ashamed of the gospel in the face of suffering.
elements.questionHint: The theme is the subject, not the message. If you had to say what the passage is ’about’ in a single phrase — before explaining what it says about it — what would that phrase be?

elements.question: MESSAGE: What is Paul saying about that theme? Complete this sentence: ‘Paul tells Timothy that…’ Aim for one or two clear sentences, without technical language, that even a child could understand.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Complete the sentence: ‘Paul tells Timothy that…’
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul tells Timothy that he should not be ashamed of the gospel or of suffering for it — because the gospel is the good news of a grace planned by God before time began, revealed in Christ who destroyed death, and because Paul himself has found that trusting God in suffering leads not to shame but to unshakeable confidence.
elements.questionHint: The message should complete the theme: the theme says ’what about?’; the message says ’what about it?’ Try to say it simply — no jargon, no technical terms. Could a ten-year-old understand your sentence?

elements.text: Step 4: BRAIN — Understand the Purpose

elements.question: PURPOSE: Why is Paul making this point to Timothy? What is he trying to achieve by saying this, in this situation, at this moment?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what Timothy needs and what danger Paul is guarding against. What would happen if Timothy did not hear this message?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul is writing to prevent Timothy from being paralysed by shame or fear in the face of suffering and opposition. Timothy is associated with an imprisoned apostle in a hostile world, and the temptation to distance himself from Paul and the gospel would have been real and powerful. Paul’s purpose is twofold: to call Timothy to courage (the command not to be ashamed), and to ground that courage in something solid — the gospel itself (vv. 9-10) and Paul’s own example of trust in God (v. 12). Without this message, Timothy might have retreated into silence or compromise. With it, he has both a command and a foundation for obedience.
elements.questionHint: The purpose answers ’why?’ — not ’what is being said?’ but ’what is the saying meant to achieve?’ What danger is Paul trying to help Timothy avoid, and what response is he trying to evoke?

elements.question: CONNECTIONS: What connections do you see between the purpose of 2 Tim 1:8-12 and your own situation and congregation today? Capture your answer as a statement or question that brings the passage into the present.
elements.answerPlaceholder: What do you and your people need to hear from this passage? Why do they need to hear it now?
elements.sampleAnswer: A connecting question might be: ‘When the gospel brings suffering or shame, where does your confidence come from?’ Like Timothy, Christians and church leaders today face situations where identifying with the gospel — and with those who suffer for it — carries a social, professional, or personal cost. The temptation to be ashamed, to soften the message, or to avoid association with unpopular aspects of the gospel is as real today as it was in the first century. The passage calls us to the same thing it called Timothy: not courage in ourselves, but confidence in the God who planned this gospel in eternity, revealed it in Christ, and can guard everything entrusted to him until the last day.
elements.questionHint: Think about the specific pressures your congregation faces. Where are they most tempted to be ’ashamed’ — at work, in their families, in public? How does the gospel foundation in vv. 9-10 speak to that specific pressure?

elements.text: Responding to 2 Timothy 1:8-12

elements.question: How did the message of 2 Tim 1:8-12 speak to you personally? What did you learn, or what did you need to be reminded of?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: How did the purpose of the passage help you to apply God’s word to your own life? What change might this passage make to your thinking or living?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: In your own words, state the message you would share with others from this passage. What is the main thing you want people to understand?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Well done for working through the four steps on your own. The more you practise them — with different passages and in different contexts — the more natural they will become. In Unit 13 we will return to 2 Tim 1:6-7 and learn how to move from encountering a passage to preaching and teaching it. Keep the habit of patient, prayerful reading: it is the foundation of everything that follows.

We have met the word ’gospel’ many times in the preceding units and seen its central importance in the Pastoral Epistles. These letters are fundamentally about the importance of being a true teacher of God’s message — the gospel — and the need to establish other true teachers of the faith. This unit builds on our encounter with 2 Timothy 1:8-12 through guided reflection in three steps: encounter (your own understanding), reflection (what Paul says), and action (what to do with what you discover).

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Reflect independently on issues you meet in the Pastoral Epistles
2. Evaluate your own understanding of the gospel
3. Explain what the gospel is to other church leaders
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.text: 6.2 Step 1: Encounter Your Own Understanding of the Gospel

Before looking again at what the Pastoral Epistles say about the gospel, we need to be honest about our own understanding — or lack of understanding — of it. Some people assume they are Christians but have not fully understood the gospel. And genuine believers often have quite different understandings of it. Use the questions below to surface your own thinking before examining Paul’s.

elements.question: What do you think the word ’gospel’ means?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Write what comes to mind — don’t look anything up yet. What does ’gospel’ mean to you in your own words?
elements.sampleAnswer: The word ’gospel’ literally means ’good news’. It is a message — an announcement — not primarily a set of instructions or a way of life, but news about something that has happened. In the New Testament it refers specifically to the good news about Jesus Christ: who he is, what he has done, and what that means for humanity. It is the announcement that God has acted decisively in history through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus to save sinners and bring them into a right relationship with himself.
elements.questionHint: Think about the word itself before thinking about its content. What kind of thing is ’good news’ — is it instructions, a story, a demand, an announcement? Then think about what makes it ’good’.

elements.question: Give a short summary and explanation of the gospel as you understand it.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to explain the gospel in 3-5 sentences — as if you were explaining it to someone who had never heard it before…
elements.sampleAnswer: God created human beings to know him and live in his world under his good rule. But we have all turned away from God — choosing our own way over his — and this rebellion has broken our relationship with him and brought death into the world. God, in his love and grace, sent his Son Jesus Christ into the world. Jesus lived the life we should have lived, died the death our sin deserved, and rose again, conquering death. Through repentance and faith in Jesus, we can be forgiven, reconciled to God, and given new life by his Spirit — now and for eternity.
elements.questionHint: A good gospel summary usually covers: the problem (why we need good news), the solution (what God has done in Christ), and the response (what it means for us). Try structuring your answer around those three movements.

elements.question: Comment on the importance of the gospel to you personally. What difference has the good news made in your own life?
elements.answerPlaceholder: This is a personal question — be honest. How has the gospel actually shaped your life, your thinking, your relationships, your ministry?
elements.sampleAnswer: Sample answers will vary significantly here, and that is by design. The question is inviting honest personal reflection rather than a ’correct’ theological answer. What matters is that the answer is genuine — reflecting real ways the gospel has (or has not yet) taken root and borne fruit in one’s actual life and ministry, rather than what one thinks the ’right’ answer should be.
elements.questionHint: Try to be specific rather than general. Instead of ’the gospel has changed everything’, try to name one or two concrete ways it has changed how you think about yourself, how you treat others, or what you do with your time and ministry.

elements.question: Comment on what the gospel seems to mean to other church leaders you know. Why do you think there are different understandings of the gospel among Christians?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the church leaders and Christians you know — do they all explain the gospel the same way? What differences have you noticed, and why might those differences exist?
elements.sampleAnswer: Different understandings of the gospel tend to cluster around a few common patterns. Some leaders emphasise the gospel primarily as moral transformation — ‘becoming a better person’. Others focus on social justice, healing, or prosperity. Others present it mainly as a set of doctrines to believe. Still others reduce it to a transaction (‘say this prayer and you will go to heaven’). These differences arise from a range of causes: incomplete Bible teaching, cultural influences that shape what people most want to hear, personal experience, and sometimes a lack of sustained engagement with what the New Testament actually says. The Pastoral Epistles were written precisely because false understandings of the gospel were damaging the church.
elements.questionHint: Think about sermons you have heard, conversations you have had, and gospel explanations you have encountered. Do they all say the same thing? If not, what are the main differences — and what might explain them?

elements.text: 6.3 Step 2: Reflect on the Gospel in the Pastoral Epistles

We have thought about our own understanding of the gospel. Now we examine what Paul actually says. We begin each time with 2 Timothy 1:8-14 before broadening our investigation into the other Pastoral Epistles.

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>So do not be ashamed of the testimony about our Lord or of me, a prisoner for his sake, but by God’s power accept your share of suffering for the gospel. <sup>9</sup>He has saved us and called us with a holy calling, not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace, granted to us in Christ Jesus before time began. <sup>10</sup>But now this has been revealed through the appearing of our Savior Christ Jesus, who abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel! <sup>11</sup>For this gospel I was appointed a preacher and apostle and teacher. <sup>12</sup>Because of this, in fact, I suffer as I do. But I am not ashamed, because I know the one in whom my faith is set and I am convinced that he is able to protect what has been entrusted to me until that day. <sup>13</sup>Hold to the standard of sound words that you heard from me and do so with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus. <sup>14</sup>Protect that good thing entrusted to you, through the Holy Spirit who lives within us.

elements.question: WORDS FOR THE GOSPEL: What words and phrases does Paul use in 2 Timothy 1:8-14 to refer to the gospel — or to mean the same thing as ’the gospel’? List them.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look carefully through the passage. Which words or phrases seem to be standing in for, or closely related to, ‘the gospel’?
elements.sampleAnswer:
In 2 Timothy 1:8-14 Paul uses a rich cluster of terms that all point to or describe the gospel:
’The testimony about our Lord’ (v. 8) — the gospel is a testimony, a witness to what Christ has done.
’The gospel’ (vv. 8, 10, 11) — used three times, each time in a slightly different context.
’His own purpose and grace’ (v. 9) — describes the divine origin of the gospel.
’Sound words’ (v. 13) — the gospel as a trustworthy, health-giving body of teaching.
’That good thing entrusted to you’ (v. 14) — the gospel as a precious deposit to be guarded.
More broadly across the Pastoral Epistles: ‘the faith’ (1 Tim 1:2), ‘the truth’ (1 Tim 2:4), ‘the trustworthy saying’ (1 Tim 1:15), ‘godliness’ (1 Tim 3:16), ‘sound doctrine’ (1 Tim 1:10; Titus 1:9). The variety of terms shows that the gospel is not a formula but a rich, many-sided reality.
elements.questionHint: Don’t just look for the word ’gospel’ itself. Look for phrases that seem to be describing the same thing — the message Paul preaches, the deposit he guards, the truth he commends. How many different ways does Paul refer to it in just these seven verses?

elements.question: FOCUS OF THE GOSPEL: What does 2 Timothy 1:8-14 tell us about what lies at the heart of the gospel? What is it fundamentally about?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look especially at vv. 9-10. What or who is the gospel centred on, and what has happened that makes it ’good news’?
elements.sampleAnswer: The heart of the gospel, according to vv. 9-10, is the person and work of Jesus Christ. Paul describes it in terms of what God has done: he has saved us, called us with a holy calling — not based on our works but on his own purpose and grace. This grace was granted to us in Christ Jesus before time began but has now been revealed through the appearing of our Saviour, Christ Jesus. The focus then narrows to two great acts of Christ: he abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel. The gospel is therefore fundamentally christological — it is about what God has done in and through Jesus. It is not primarily about human response, human effort, or moral improvement; it is about a decisive divine act.
elements.questionHint: Verses 9-10 contain a compressed gospel summary. Try to identify: (1) what God has done, (2) on what basis he did it, (3) when it was planned, (4) when it was revealed, and (5) what Christ specifically accomplished. Each of these tells you something about the focus of the gospel.

elements.question: FOUNDATION OF THE GOSPEL: What does 2 Timothy 1:8-14 tell us about who or what lies behind the gospel? Where does it ultimately come from?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the origin of the gospel — who planned it, who initiated it, and whose power and purpose stand behind it?
elements.sampleAnswer: The foundation of the gospel is entirely God himself. Paul is emphatic that the gospel did not originate with human beings and does not rest on human merit or initiative. It is grounded in ’his own purpose and grace’ (v. 9) — not because of anything we have done, but because of God’s sovereign, gracious will. The grace that lies behind the gospel was ’granted to us in Christ Jesus before time began’ (v. 9) — before creation, before human history, before any human action. The gospel is therefore rooted in the eternal purpose of a God who is faithful and who does not change. This is why Paul can trust God to ’protect what has been entrusted’ (v. 12) — the gospel’s security rests on God’s own character.
elements.questionHint: Look at v. 9 carefully. Paul uses two contrasting phrases to describe the basis of salvation. What are they, and what does that contrast tell you about where the gospel comes from and where it does not come from?

elements.question: FRUIT OF THE GOSPEL: What does 2 Timothy 1:8-14 tell us about what the gospel produces — in the world, in Paul, and in Timothy?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at what the gospel has produced in Paul’s life and ministry, what it has done in the world, and what it is meant to produce in Timothy…
elements.sampleAnswer:
The passage points to several fruits of the gospel:
In the world: the gospel abolishes death and brings life and immortality to light (v. 10) — it has cosmic, not merely personal, effects.
In Paul: the gospel produces a calling to ministry as preacher, apostle, and teacher (v. 11), and a willingness to suffer for it (v. 12). It also produces the unashamed confidence expressed in v. 12 — ‘I know the one in whom my faith is set.’ The gospel is the source of Paul’s courage.
In Timothy: the gospel is meant to produce the same unashamed courage (v. 8), a commitment to holding to sound words (v. 13), and a faithful guarding of the good deposit through the Holy Spirit (v. 14). The gospel is not merely believed — it is to be lived, suffered for, guarded, and passed on.
elements.questionHint: Look for what changes when the gospel arrives — in Paul’s life, in the world, and in what Paul expects of Timothy. The gospel is not just news to be heard; it is a power that produces fruit. What fruit does Paul identify here?

elements.text: The Gospel and Time: Past, Present, and Future

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people. <sup>12</sup>It trains us to reject godless ways and worldly desires and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age, <sup>13</sup>as we wait for the happy fulfillment of our hope in the glorious appearing of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ. <sup>14</sup>He gave himself for us to set us free from every kind of lawlessness and to purify for himself a people who are his very own, who are eager to do good.
